papers.title: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences
papers.authors: Christiano, Leike, Brown, Martic, Legg, Amodei
papers.abstract: The paper that turned human preference into a training signal: fit a reward model to a non-expert's pairwise comparisons of 1–2 second behavior clips (a Bradley–Terry preference predictor), then optimize it online with deep RL (A2C for Atari, TRPO for MuJoCo). It solves Atari and simulated locomotion without ever seeing the true reward — feedback on under 1% of the agent's interactions: ~700 comparisons to match true-reward RL across eight MuJoCo tasks and 5,500 on seven Atari games — and instills behaviors with no writable reward, like a Hopper backflip from ~900 queries in under an hour. Offline (frozen) reward training collapses into reward hacking (Pong's infinite volleys), so feedback must stay online. The direct ancestor of RLHF and an early demonstration of reward over-optimization.

papers.title: AdvancedIF: Rubric-Based Benchmarking and Reinforcement Learning for Advancing LLM Instruction Following
papers.authors: He, Li, Zhang, …, Awadalla, Faruqui
papers.abstract: A 1,645-prompt instruction-following benchmark whose prompts and rubrics are entirely human-expert-written, spanning complex single-turn, multi-turn carried-context, and system-prompt steerability, and kept hard by adversarially retaining only model-failing prompts. Even GPT-5 (77.9) and Gemini 3 Pro (74.7) top out around 75-78%, with reasoning effort clearly helping and multi-turn / system steering lagging single-turn complexity. The companion pipeline RIFL reuses the rubrics as an RL reward: a fine-tuned rubric generator (F1 0.790), a two-stage SFT+RL rubric verifier (human-agreement F1 0.515->0.728, matching o3-mini's 0.723), and an all-or-nothing reward with two anti-reward-hacking criteria, lifting Llama 4 Maverick +6.7 on AdvancedIF (51.4->58.1), +2.9 on the out-of-distribution MultiChallenge, and ~flat on the saturated IFEval.

papers.title: SWE Atlas: Benchmarking Coding Agents Beyond Issue Resolution
papers.authors: Raghavendra, Dan, Romero Calvo, …, He
papers.abstract: A 284-task benchmark covering the software-engineering work that issue-resolution benchmarks ignore — Codebase Q&A, Test Writing, and Refactoring — across 18 real repos. It pairs programmatic checks with rubric-based LLM-as-judge grading and finds engineering quality lags functional correctness by 15–40 points even for frontier models (GPT-5.4 and Opus 4.7 lead at ~42–43% Pass@1).

papers.title: SciPredict: Can LLMs Predict the Outcomes of Scientific Experiments in Natural Sciences?
papers.authors: Sehwag, Lau, Ehsani Oskouie, …, Huang, Liu
papers.abstract: A 405-task benchmark drawn from physics, chemistry, and biology papers posted after March 2025 (no pretraining leakage, web search disabled) that asks 15 frontier LLMs to predict experimental outcomes. Models score just 14–26% — roughly matching ~20% human experts — but unlike experts, whose accuracy climbs from ~5% to ~80% as they judge a question more predictable, models stay flat at ~20% regardless of their own confidence, exposing a calibration gap that makes them untrustworthy for experiment planning.

papers.title: MoReBench: Evaluating Procedural and Pluralistic Moral Reasoning in Language Models, More than Outcomes
papers.authors: Chiu, Lee, Calcott, …, Choi, Gordon, Levine
papers.abstract: A process-focused moral-reasoning benchmark: 1,000 dilemmas paired with 23,018 criteria written by 53 moral philosophers, grading how a model's thinking trace reasons rather than which side it lands on. Across 14 frontier reasoning models, moral-reasoning skill is nearly uncorrelated with scaling and with math/code/Arena benchmarks (Pearson r between −0.245 and 0.216); models reliably avoid harm (72–85%) but are weakest at integrating competing considerations, and reason markedly better in Utilitarian/Deontological frames than in Virtue Ethics or Contractarianism. A sibling set, MoReBench-Theory (150 scenarios), tests reasoning within five normative frameworks.

papers.title: MCP-Atlas: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Tool-Use Competency with Real MCP Servers
papers.authors: Bandi, Dumitru, Hertzberg, …, Kenstler, Liu
papers.abstract: A 1,000-task benchmark over 36 production MCP servers and 220 real tools, scored by an answer-centric claims rubric that credits any valid tool path; distractors are baked in so 98.6% of tasks demand genuine tool discovery across two or more servers. Across 20 frontier models the agentic frontier is no longer tool-mechanics-bound — Muse Spark leads at 82.2% Pass@0.75 and open-weight GLM-5.1 reaches 75.6% — but 63.3% of ~6,900 diagnosed failures are cognitive (premature stopping, faulty synthesis) rather than tool-call errors, and o3 Pro lands at 44.5% by issuing no tool call on 40% of its failed tasks.

papers.title: PRBench: Large-Scale Expert Rubrics for Evaluating High-Stakes Professional Reasoning
papers.authors: Akyürek, Gosai, Zhang, …, Liu, He
papers.abstract: The largest public rubric-based benchmark for law and finance: 1,100 expert-authored tasks and 19,356 weighted criteria from 182 credentialed professionals (JD/CFA/6+ yrs) spanning 114 countries and 47 US jurisdictions. Across 20 frontier models the best score only 0.39 (Finance) and 0.37 (Legal) on the Hard subsets — Instruction Following is near-solved while Supplemental Insight and Handling Uncertainty lag, models with similar overall scores diverge sharply by capability, and the dominant failure modes are inaccurate judgments, missing process transparency, and incomplete reasoning.

papers.title: ResearchRubrics: A Benchmark of Prompts and Rubrics For Evaluating Deep Research Agents
papers.authors: Sharma, Zhang, Bandi, …, Kenstler, Liu
papers.abstract: A fully human-built benchmark for Deep Research agents: 101 open-ended prompts paired with 2,593 expert-written, weighted rubric criteria (2,800+ hours of labor) across nine domains, scored by LLM-as-judge over three-stage expert review. Even the strongest agents — Gemini DR (0.677) and OpenAI DR (0.664) — clear under 68% of criteria under ternary grading, with no system topping 70%; failures concentrate in implicit reasoning and cross-document synthesis (45–50% of all failures) rather than factual retrieval. Methodologically, binary grading aligns with human judges ~20 points better than ternary partial credit (0.76 vs 0.57 Macro-F1), and LLM-augmenting the expert rubrics degrades alignment by 15–20%.

papers.title: Remote Labor Index: Measuring AI Automation of Remote Work
papers.authors: Mazeika, Gatti, Menghini, …, Yue, Wang, Hendrycks
papers.abstract: The first economically grounded measure of AI automation: 240 real freelance projects sourced from verified Upwork professionals — over 6,000 hours of work valued at $143,991, spanning 23 categories from video and CAD to game development — each paired with the human gold-standard deliverable and graded by holistic manual review (94.4% inter-annotator agreement). Frontier agents perform near the floor: the best, Manus, completes just 2.5% of projects to a client-acceptable standard and earns $1,720 of the bundle, with the rest between 0.8–2.1%. An Elo metric (human baseline = 1,000; best AI ≈ 510) and an 'autoflation' price index register steady relative progress even as absolute end-to-end automation stays near zero, and failures cluster on execution — poor quality (45.6%), incomplete deliverables (35.7%), corrupted files (17.6%) — rather than deep reasoning.

papers.title: Online Rubrics Elicitation from Pairwise Comparisons
papers.authors: Rezaei, Vacareanu, Z. Wang, C. Wang, Liu, He, Akyürek
papers.abstract: Makes the RL rubric a living object: instead of grading long-form answers against a fixed, hand-written checklist, OnlineRubrics elicits new weighted criteria online by contrasting the current policy's responses with a control model's, closing the seams that let static rubrics be reward-hacked. Training Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct with GRPO, it adds up to +8 points over human-rubric-only training (and +25 over the base model) on AlpacaEval, Arena-Hard, and GPQA, with pairwise extraction beating pointwise by 6–7 points — though gains concentrate on open-ended, LLM-judged quality while verifiable GSM8K/GPQA barely move.

papers.title: SWE-Bench Pro: Can AI Agents Solve Long-Horizon Software Engineering Tasks?
papers.authors: Deng, Da, Pan, …, Liu, Kenstler
papers.abstract: A contamination-resistant hardening of SWE-bench: 1,865 human-verified, multi-file coding tasks (reference patches average 107.4 LOC across 4.1 files) from 41 actively-maintained repos, split into an open public set (731, GPL/copyleft), a private held-out set (858), and a commercial set (276) purchased from startups. Under one SWE-Agent scaffold the best agents clear under 45% on the public set (Claude Sonnet 4.5 43.6%, Sonnet 4 42.7%, GPT-5 high 41.8%) and collapse to 9–18% on the proprietary commercial repos (Opus 4.1 17.8%); a $2/50-turn budget cap roughly halves every score (GPT-5 25.9%, Opus 4.1 22.7%). Human-written requirements and interface specs triple solvability (≈8%→23–26%), and on these long-horizon tasks the dominant failure is agents exhausting context before ever submitting a patch — GPT-5 fails to submit on 72.8% of its misses, 96.4% from long context.

papers.title: TutorBench: A Benchmark To Assess Tutoring Capabilities Of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Srinivasa, Che, Zhang, …, Gunjal, He, Liu, Xing
papers.abstract: A 1,490-sample, 15,220-criterion benchmark of high-school and AP-level STEM tutoring across six subjects — adaptive explanation, feedback on student work, and hint generation — with 828 multimodal samples (photos of handwritten work) graded by a Claude Sonnet 4 judge against self-contained, sample-specific expert rubrics, kept hard by discarding any item three of five strong models could already solve. Across 16 frontier models none clears 56% (Gemini 2.5 Pro 55.65%, GPT-5 55.33%, o3 Pro 54.62%); adaptive explanation is the weakest use case (47.16% average), models are best at spotting correct/incorrect student steps (~53%) but worst at offering examples or analogies (32.8%), and the Claude family leads active-learning support (0.58–0.59) while trailing overall. Notably GPT-5 Study Mode scores just 46.94% — below vanilla GPT-5 — because its withholding, question-asking style is penalized by single-final-response grading.

papers.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
papers.authors: Jimenez, Yang, Wettig, …, Press, Narasimhan
papers.abstract: The benchmark that re-grounded code evaluation in real software engineering: 2,294 task instances mined from merged GitHub pull requests across 12 Python repositories, where a model must patch a codebase to resolve an issue and is graded by running the repo's own hidden unit tests (the patch must apply and all fail-to-pass + pass-to-pass tests must pass). In 2023 the best models resolved under 4% (Claude 3 Opus 3.79%, Claude 2 1.96%) — localization in long contexts, not editing, was the wall, with oracle retrieval roughly doubling scores. Its execution-graded, contamination-resistant, continually-refreshable format became the foundation of the coding-agent era (SWE-agent, SWE-bench Verified, SWE-Bench Pro).

papers.title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
papers.authors: Chen, Tworek, Jun, …, Sutskever, Zaremba
papers.abstract: OpenAI fine-tunes GPT on public GitHub Python (Codex) and introduces HumanEval — 164 hand-written problems graded by executing hidden unit tests rather than matching reference text. Codex-12B solves 28.8% from a single sample and 70.2% with 100 samples (Codex-S reaches 77.5% with an oracle pick, 44.5% by mean-logprob reranking); the paper establishes functional correctness and the unbiased pass@k estimator as the standard for code evaluation.

papers.title: Insights Generator: Systematic Corpus-Level Trace Diagnostics for LLM Agents
papers.authors: Manglik, Shanker, Deshpande, …, Lentz, Xue
papers.abstract: A multi-agent system for corpus-level trace diagnostics: instead of debugging an LLM agent one trace at a time, IG runs a scout/investigator loop that proposes behavioral hypotheses on a sample and validates each statistically across the whole trace corpus — routed through a stateful Python layer so it scales past any context window — emitting an evidence-backed report. Across SpreadsheetBench and HLE a Claude Opus 4.6 judge ranks IG first on pairwise report quality (77.9% win rate vs 62.4% next), and — the headline — professional engineers handed an IG report improved a held-out scaffold by 30.4 pp over baseline (57.4% vs 27.0%), nearly doubling the next-best report's 16.2 pp gain (p≈0.016, n=6). Under fully-autonomous patching, though, a 3×-cheaper recursive baseline matches IG's 0.81–0.84 terminal pass rate.

papers.title: HealthBench: Evaluating Large Language Models Towards Improved Human Health
papers.authors: Arora, Wei, Soskin Hicks, …, Heidecke, Singhal
papers.abstract: An open-source health benchmark: 5,000 realistic multi-turn conversations graded against 48,562 conversation-specific rubric criteria written by 262 physicians across 60 countries, scored by a validated GPT-4.1 grader instead of multiple choice. Frontier performance climbs from 0.16 (GPT-3.5 Turbo) to 0.60 (o3) — with HealthBench Hard topping out at 0.32 — GPT-4.1 nano beats August 2024's GPT-4o at 1/25th the cost, and recent models now out-write unassisted physicians (0.13) while the model grader matches physician-level agreement (Macro-F1 0.709). A safety-critical, meta-evaluated, deliberately unsaturated rubric benchmark.

papers.title: Not Every Rubric Teaches Equally: Policy-Aware Rubric Rewards for RLVR
papers.authors: Tyagi, Guo, Rezaei, George, Mahmoud, Lee, Liu, He
papers.abstract: A policy-aware rubric reward for RLVR. Static weighted-sum rubrics route 45–51% of GRPO's within-category training pressure onto saturated or dead criteria that can't move the policy (17–26% of criteria are saturated and 20–33% dead for a fresh policy), because a criterion every rollout passes or fails cancels out of the standardized advantage. POW3R keeps the human weights and category balance as the fixed evaluation target but reweights within-category pressure toward criteria that currently separate rollouts, via a bounded EMA factor derived from judge-verdict variance. Across six base policies (Qwen3-VL, Gemma 3) on a 10k-task multimodal set and HealthBench it wins 24 of 30 base-policy/metric comparisons, Pareto-dominates on mean rubric reward and strict completion, and reaches the same plateau in 2.5–4× fewer GRPO steps — though the dynamic factor is an increment over category-balancing and every number is mediated by GPT-5.4 judges.

papers.title: MLE-bench: Evaluating Machine Learning Agents on Machine Learning Engineering
papers.authors: Chan, Chowdhury, Jaffe, …, Patwardhan, Weng, Mądry
papers.abstract: OpenAI's benchmark of autonomous ML engineering: 75 Kaggle competitions hand-ported into an offline environment and graded against the real human leaderboards via Kaggle's bronze/silver/gold medal system. The best agent — o1-preview with the AIDE scaffold — earns a medal in 16.9% of competitions (34.1% at pass@8), with scaffolding mattering as much as the model (AIDE 8.7% vs MLAB 0.8% on GPT-4o) and agents failing more on engineering — valid submissions, debugging, compute budgeting — than on ML. Contamination probes (familiarity r=−0.24; obfuscation 8.5%→8.4%) find no systematic inflation, and the benchmark is named as an autonomy / AI-R&D dial in OpenAI's Preparedness, Anthropic's RSP, and DeepMind's Frontier Safety frameworks.

papers.title: ASPI: Seeking Ambiguity Clarification Amplifies Prompt Injection Vulnerability in LLM Agents
papers.authors: Sehwag, Shan, H. Liu, Lakshan, Brandifino, Fenkell
papers.abstract: Clarification-seeking is treated as a virtue in LLM agents, but ASPI shows it is also an attack surface. Building 728 paired task–attack scenarios on AgentDojo's four domains, it holds the task, attacker goal, environment, and scoring fixed while varying only the interaction state and the channel the injection arrives through. Across ten frontier models, the same attack that bounces off an agent during execution lands once the agent solicits input: attack success rises from 1.8%→34.0% for o3, 2.2%→35.7% for Gemini-3-Flash, and 11.1%→63.1% for Kimi K2.5, while Claude-Opus-4.7 stays flat (2.1%→2.2%). A state-vs-channel decomposition pins the solicited-response ask_user channel as the primary driver, lightweight prompt-guard/tool-filter defenses dent but don't close the gap (Gemini-3-Flash 35.7%→23.9%), and because the design uses minimal single-slot, one-round ambiguity the reported rates are a floor.

papers.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
papers.authors: Wei, Wang, Schuurmans, …, Le, Zhou
papers.abstract: Adding a few hand-written reasoning exemplars to a prompt — no finetuning — elicits multi-step 'chain-of-thought' reasoning that scaling alone never unlocked. Eight exemplars take PaLM 540B from 17.9% to 56.9% on GSM8K, beating a finetuned GPT-3 with a verifier; the gain is an emergent ability of scale (~100B+), generalizes to commonsense (StrategyQA 75.6% vs 69.4% SOTA) and symbolic length-generalization tasks, and ablations pin it on natural-language sequential reasoning rather than extra compute or knowledge activation. The paper that made the reasoning trace a first-class, inspectable object.

papers.title: Reward Hacking in Rubric-Based Reinforcement Learning
papers.authors: Mahmoud, Rezaei, Wang, Gunjal, Liu, He
papers.abstract: A diagnostic framework for reward hacking in rubric-based RL: a policy is trained against a single verifier (proxy reward) but evaluated against a cross-family panel of three frontier judges (reference reward), separating verifier failure from rubric-design limits. Under a weak verifier (GPT-4o-mini), proxy reward rises while panel reward plateaus and the exploitation rate climbs 39%→65% (medical) / 63%→75% (science) — confirmed externally on HealthBench — while a strong verifier (GPT-OSS-120B) curbs but doesn't kill it (~7× less exploitation). A verifier-free self-internalization gap from policy log-probs tracks reference quality (r 0.91–0.97) as a stopping signal. The deeper failure: even under strong verification, rubric-based judges prefer the RL checkpoint (85.8%) while rubric-free judges prefer the base model (78.4%), because 90.2% of rubric weight is presence-based vs 8.6% absence-based — so RL buys completeness (+14.9 pp) while shedding conciseness, relevance, and factual correctness.

papers.title: Aligning AI With Shared Human Values
papers.authors: Hendrycks, Burns, Basart, …, Song, Steinhardt
papers.abstract: Introduces ETHICS, a 130,000+ example benchmark that probes a model's grasp of basic morality across five normative frameworks — justice, deontology, virtue ethics, utilitarianism, and commonsense — by predicting the moral judgments people widely agree on about open-world natural-language scenarios. In 2020 fine-tuned Transformers reached 71.0% average on the Test set but only 47.9% on an adversarially-filtered Hard Test (ALBERT-xxlarge), with GPT-3 few-shot trailing at 39.3/32.3 — promising but incomplete. Examples are kept clean via consensus relabeling, contrast-set augmentation, and adversarial filtration, and a held-out set probes whether models can flag moral disagreement (they mostly can't, AUROC 56–69%). The outcome-label moral-values eval that process-focused benchmarks like MoReBench were later built to move beyond.

papers.title: MCP-Universe: Benchmarking Large Language Models with Real-World Model Context Protocol Servers
papers.authors: Luo, Shen, Yang, …, Xiong, Li
papers.abstract: The first benchmark to point LLM agents at real, live MCP servers — Google Maps, GitHub, Yahoo Finance, Blender, Playwright — across 231 hand-built tasks over 11 servers (133 tools), graded by 84 execution-based format/static/dynamic evaluators rather than an LLM judge (the 57.1% dynamic evaluators auto-refetch live ground truth so time-sensitive answers stay gradable). Even GPT-5 clears only 43.72% (Grok-4 33.33%, Claude-4.0-Sonnet 29.44%, best open-weight GLM-4.5 24.68%); Location Navigation defeats everyone (<35%), failures concentrate in content over format, and the two named walls are long-context blow-up (~90k tokens by step 16) and unfamiliar tool interfaces — while enterprise scaffolds like Cursor don't beat plain ReAct.

papers.title: Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena
papers.authors: Zheng, Chiang, Sheng, …, Gonzalez, Stoica
papers.abstract: Establishes LLM-as-a-judge — using a strong model like GPT-4 to grade open-ended chat answers — and validates it against two new human-preference benchmarks: MT-bench (80 hand-written multi-turn questions across 8 categories) and Chatbot Arena (crowdsourced anonymous battles, ~30K votes from 2,114 IPs). GPT-4 judges agree with expert humans 85% of the time on non-tie votes — above the 81% humans agree with each other (87% on Arena) — while the paper documents and mitigates position, verbosity, and self-enhancement biases (only GPT-4 stays >60% position-consistent and resists a verbosity attack at 8.7% vs ~91% for weaker judges). The work made model-graded preference evaluation respectable and seeded the LMSYS leaderboard lineage.

papers.title: LegalBench: A Collaboratively Built Benchmark for Measuring Legal Reasoning in Large Language Models
papers.authors: Guha, Nyarko, Ho, Ré, …, Henderson, Goel
papers.abstract: A collaboratively built legal-reasoning benchmark: 162 tasks across 36 corpora, hand-crafted by 40 legal professionals and organized by the IRAC framework (issue-spotting, rule-recall, rule-application, rule-conclusion) plus interpretation and rhetorical-understanding, scored mostly by balanced-accuracy with rule-application graded manually by a law-trained expert. Across 20 LLMs from 11 families GPT-4 leads but unevenly (rule-conclusion 89.9, rule-recall 59.2), Claude-1 rivals it only on rule-recall, and open Flan-T5-XXL beats GPT-3.5/Claude-1 on issue-spotting and rhetorical tasks; explanation 'analysis' lags answer correctness and plain-vs-technical prompt phrasing swings scores up to 21 points. The expert-authored, discipline-native-typology template later rubric benchmarks (PRBench, HealthBench, ResearchRubrics) scaled up.

papers.title: FinanceBench: A New Benchmark for Financial Question Answering
papers.authors: Islam, Kannappan, Kiela, Qian, Scherrer, Vidgen
papers.abstract: The first ecologically-valid, open-book financial-QA benchmark: 10,231 expert-written (question, answer, evidence) triplets over 361 real SEC filings from 40 US public companies (2015–2023), graded by manual review on a 150-case open-sourced slice (n=2,400 responses). Across 16 configurations of GPT-4/Turbo, Claude 2, and Llama 2, a realistic shared-vector-store setup leaves GPT-4-Turbo at just 19% correct (81% wrong or refused) and even the best long-context config reaches 79%; the single-store→shared-store→oracle ladder shows the wall is retrieval, not reasoning, while Llama 2 confidently hallucinates (70% incorrect) where GPT-4-Turbo refuses.

papers.title: GDPval: Evaluating AI Model Performance on Real-World Economically Valuable Tasks
papers.authors: Patwardhan, Dias, Proehl, …, Barr, Glaese, Tworek
papers.abstract: OpenAI's benchmark of real, economically valuable knowledge work: 1,320 tasks across 44 occupations in the 9 largest U.S. GDP sectors, authored by industry experts (avg 14 years' experience) and graded by blinded expert pairwise comparison against the human's own deliverable rather than an answer key, with 220 gold tasks open-sourced plus a public automated grader (65.7% vs 70.8% inter-human agreement). Frontier models approach but don't reach parity — Claude Opus 4.1 leads at 47.6% wins+ties and GPT-5 high at 38.8%, on a roughly linear upward trend — while a realistic 'try the model, then fix it yourself' workflow saves only ~1.4× time/cost for GPT-5 and ~3% of GPT-5's losses are rated catastrophic.

papers.title: BrowseComp: A Simple Yet Challenging Benchmark for Browsing Agents
papers.authors: Wei, Sun, Papay, …, Fedus, Glaese
papers.abstract: OpenAI's browsing-agent benchmark: 1,266 short-answer questions built by inverting a known fact so the answer is easy to verify but extremely hard to find — each vetted to stump GPT-4o/o1/an early Deep Research and to resist five Google searches. Non-browsing models score near zero (GPT-4o 0.6%, GPT-4.5 0.9%) and bolting on browsing barely helps (1.9%), while reasoning alone lifts o1 to 9.9% and an early Deep Research agent — trained for persistent browsing — solves 51.5%, scaling smoothly with test-time compute and reaching ~78% via best-of-N over 64 samples. Human trainers gave up on 70.8% of questions after two hours; the deliberate trade-off is that BrowseComp measures persistence and creative search for entangled facts, not long-form answers or query ambiguity, and browsing actually worsens calibration (Deep Research 91% error).

papers.title: DeepResearch Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Deep Research Agents
papers.authors: Du, Xu, Zhu, Wang, Mao
papers.abstract: The first benchmark built specifically for Deep Research Agents: 100 PhD-level tasks across 22 domains (50 English / 50 Chinese), with the per-domain task counts grounded in 44,019 filtered real user queries (from 96,147 raw). Two human-aligned frameworks grade it — RACE scores report quality against a high-quality reference report using dynamically-weighted, judge-generated adaptive criteria, and FACT scores citation accuracy plus effective-citation count via per-claim web verification. Gemini-2.5-Pro Deep Research leads RACE Overall (48.88) and dominates volume at 111.21 effective citations per report, but Perplexity DR cites most accurately (90.24% vs Gemini's 81.44%) and OpenAI DR (46.98) edges Gemini on Instruction-Following. RACE's 71.33% pairwise agreement with experts beats the 68.44% the experts agree with each other — though the whole pipeline is a Gemini judge scoring against a Gemini-written reference, a built-in self-preference risk.

papers.title: SWE-Lancer: Can Frontier LLMs Earn $1 Million from Real-World Freelance Software Engineering?
papers.authors: Miserendino, Wang, Patwardhan, Heidecke
papers.abstract: OpenAI's economically-grounded coding benchmark: 1,488 real freelance tasks from Upwork's open-source Expensify repository, worth $1,000,000 in actual payouts ($250–$32,000 each), split into Individual-Contributor coding tasks (764, $414,775) graded by ~100 engineers' triple-verified end-to-end Playwright browser tests, and SWE Manager tasks (724, $585,225) where the model picks among the freelancers' real proposals (99% agreement with the original engineering managers' choices). The best model — Claude 3.5 Sonnet — earns $403,325 of $1M (and $208,050 of $500,800 on the public SWE-Lancer Diamond split), resolving just 26.2% of IC coding issues; IC SWE pass@1 stays under 30% for every model while the easier proposal-picking runs ~2× higher (37–45%). Higher reasoning effort and more attempts both help (o1 climbs 16.5%→48.5% from pass@1 to pass@7), agents localize fast but fail to root-cause, and a model-first / freelancer-fallback workflow already saves o1 ~13–33% of cost — mapping capability to real economic value.

papers.title: Coverage, Not Averages: Semantic Stratification for Trustworthy Retrieval Evaluation
papers.authors: Klearman, Revutchi, Garg, …, Denton, Xue
papers.abstract: Reframes retrieval evaluation as a stratified statistical estimation problem: standard BEIR-style query sets leave 40–50% of a corpus's semantic regions untested (NFCorpus covers only 50.9% of clusters; 26 clusters hold 17.3% of documents but 1.1% of queries — e.g. immune cells, 122 docs / 1 query) and those gaps are precisely where retrieval is weakest, so aggregate nDCG overstates quality with irreducible bias. The fix organizes a corpus into entity-based Leiden clusters, defines difficulty strata via relevance dispersion (∆, governs ranking) and query–document alignment (J, governs coverage) spanning a ~3× performance range, and runs a coverage-aware LLM curation loop that lifts semantic coverage to ~90–100%. Stratified evaluation flips system-selection decisions: text-embedding-3-small beats Cohere embed-english-v3.0 in just 32.4% of bootstrap samples under query-weighted mean but 62.5% under cluster-weighted macro-average.

papers.title: OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Open-Ended Tasks in Real Computer Environments
papers.authors: Xie, Zhang, Chen, …, Xiong, Zhong, Yu
papers.abstract: The first scalable, real-computer environment for multimodal agents: a VM-backed benchmark of 369 execution-graded tasks across Ubuntu/Windows/macOS spanning web and desktop apps, OS file I/O, and multi-app workflows, each with an initial-state config and a custom evaluation script (302 initial states, 134 unique evaluation functions). The action space is raw pyautogui keyboard/mouse code with WAIT/FAIL/DONE meta-actions, graded by an execution-based reward on the final machine state. Humans solve 72.36% but the best 2024 agent (GPT-4 reading an accessibility tree) only 12.24% — collapsing to ~7% on multi-app workflows and ~5% screenshot-only — and over 75% of 550 failed runs are mouse-click/grounding errors despite sound plans, exposing GUI grounding and operational knowledge, not reasoning, as the wall. It became the substrate of the computer-use agent era.

papers.title: Reinforcement Learning with Rubric Anchors
papers.authors: Huang, Zhuang, Lu, …, Li, Zhao
papers.abstract: Extends RLVR beyond verifiable math/code by turning weighted, model-readable rubrics into the reward, building the largest rubric reward bank to date (10,000+ rubrics from humans, LLMs, or hybrid) and training an open-sourced Qwen3-30B-A3B model (Rubicon). With only ~5K samples it gains +5.2% on open-ended humanities benchmarks (Judgemark v2 +13.0, EQ-Bench3 +6.2) — edging past a 671B DeepSeek-V3 by +2.4% — while keeping general ability roughly flat (AIME24 +4.2 but LiveCodeBench v5 −4.3, reasoning avg −0.4). It names two failure modes: the 'seesaw effect' (creativity vs instruction-following objectives conflict, fixed by multi-stage RL) and reward hacking via sycophancy/self-praise, countered by a veto-style defense rubric. Reported as a single-run preview with the rubric bank and aggregation recipe unreleased.

papers.title: Checklists Are Better Than Reward Models For Aligning Language Models
papers.authors: Viswanathan, Sun, Ma, Kong, Cao, Neubig, Wu
papers.abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Checklist Feedback (RLCF): rather than train a single scalar reward model, it extracts an instruction-specific checklist of weighted yes/no requirements (the released WildChecklists set, ~130k instructions), grades each item with an LM judge (Qwen2.5-72B, averaged over 25 samples) combined with a confidence-gated verifier program, and turns the weighted satisfaction score into DPO preference pairs. On Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct it is the only method to improve on all five instruction-following benchmarks — +6.0 on InFoBench overall (78.1→84.1), +3.9 average hard-satisfaction-rate on FollowBench (71.4→75.3), +3.3 on Arena-Hard win rate — while reward models that top RewardBench (Skywork-27B, ArmoRM) help on InFoBench but hurt on IFEval/FollowBench, evidence that static RM accuracy is a poor proxy for RL-teaching quality. Candidate-based checklists beat prompting the instruction directly and the recipe transfers off-policy to Llama 3.1 8B and OLMo 2 7B, but it costs ~1 point on GSM8K/TruthfulQA and ~4 days of 8×H100 grading.

papers.title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models
papers.authors: Shao, Wang, Zhu, Xu, Song, …, Wu, Guo
papers.abstract: DeepSeek's open math model: continual-trained from a 7B code base on 120B math tokens mined from Common Crawl by an iterative fastText bootstrap, it reaches 64.2% GSM8K / 36.2% MATH as a base model — matching the closed Minerva 540B at ~1/77th the size — and 88.2% / 51.7% after RL, the first open model past 50% on competition MATH without tools or voting. Its lasting contribution is GRPO, a critic-free PPO variant that drops the value model and estimates the advantage baseline from a group of sampled answers per question, halving RL memory; it is now the default algorithm under most RLVR and rubric-reward training. The paper also shows its RL lifts Maj@K but not Pass@K — sharpening the output distribution rather than raising the capability ceiling.

papers.title: DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
papers.authors: Guo, Yang, Zhang, …, Shao, Li, Gao
papers.abstract: Shows reasoning can be incentivized by pure RL rather than imitated: trained directly on DeepSeek-V3-Base (671B MoE) with GRPO and a rule-based accuracy+format reward — no supervised fine-tuning, no human reasoning traces — DeepSeek-R1-Zero teaches itself to reason, with self-verification, reflection, and an 'aha moment' emerging on their own (AIME 2024 pass@1 climbs 15.6%→77.9%, 86.7% with cons@16; response length grows to >10,000 tokens unprompted). The released multi-stage DeepSeek-R1 adds cold-start SFT, rejection-sampling SFT, and preference/safety reward models to buy back readability and generality, landing on par with OpenAI o1-1217 on math and code (AIME 79.8 vs 79.2, MATH-500 97.3, LiveCodeBench 65.9, 96.3rd Codeforces percentile) while distilling into a 1.5B model that out-reasons GPT-4o. The first reasoning model peer-reviewed in Nature, and the reference point for the RLVR wave.

papers.title: BigCodeBench: Benchmarking Code Generation with Diverse Function Calls and Complex Instructions
papers.authors: Zhuo, Vu, Chim, …, de Vries, von Werra
papers.abstract: An execution-graded benchmark of 1,140 Python tasks that each require composing multiple function calls from 139 real libraries (723 calls across 7 domains) under complex instructions, graded by ≥5 unittest cases at 99% branch coverage. Two splits — Complete (full docstring) and Instruct (terse natural language) — are built by a year-long human–LLM pipeline (GPT-4 synthesis + Code-Interpreter refactoring under 20 annotators). Across 60 LLMs the best, GPT-4o, reaches only ~60% Pass@1 on Complete and under 50% on Instruct, far below the 97% human pass rate; terse instructions cost 8.5% on average and long prompts trigger 'laziness' (models omitting given imports). A difficulty-filtered BigCodeBench-Hard subset serves as a user-centric proxy once the full set saturates.

papers.title: HiL-Bench (Human-in-Loop Benchmark): Do Agents Know When to Ask for Help?
papers.authors: Elfeki, Trinh, Luo, Luu, Hunt, …, Castillo, Liu
papers.abstract: A 300-task benchmark (150 SWE from SWE-Bench Pro, 150 text-to-SQL from BIRD) that measures selective escalation — whether agents know when to ask for help. Tasks are pre-filtered so top models hit ~85% pass@3 with full information, then seeded with 3–5 human-validated blockers (42% missing, 36% ambiguous, 22% contradictory) that surface only through progressive exploration. The core metric, Ask-F1 (harmonic mean of question precision and blocker recall), architecturally defeats question spam. Across frontier models a large universal judgment gap appears: 75–89% full-info pass@3 collapses to 4–24% when agents must decide whether to ask (best Ask-F1 ~53% for GPT-5.5). Failure has a stable per-model fingerprint — GPT executes confidently on wrong beliefs, Claude detects uncertainty but submits anyway, Gemini is externally correctable only on SQL — and RLVR on shaped Ask-F1 lifts a Qwen3-32B's Ask-F1 +28pp (SQL)/+17pp (SWE) with pass@3 in lockstep and positive cross-domain transfer, showing judgment is trainable.

papers.title: Defensive Refusal Bias: How Safety Alignment Fails Cyber Defenders
papers.authors: Campbell, Kale, Sehwag, …, Levinson, Knight
papers.abstract: The first systematic study of 'Defensive Refusal Bias': across 2,390 real single-turn blue-team prompts from the National Collegiate Cyber Defense Competition, three aligned LLMs refuse legitimate defensive requests 12.2% of the time overall and 2.72× more often when the prompt carries security-sensitive vocabulary (30.5% vs 11.2%, χ²=37.3, p<0.001). The most operationally critical tasks fare worst — system hardening 43.8%, malware analysis 34.3%, vulnerability assessment 22.7% — and the most safety-tuned model (Claude 3.5 Sonnet) refuses 19.5% of requests, ~3× the open-weight Llama-3.3-70B (6.6%). Paradoxically, explicit authorization ('I'm on the blue team', 'this is for NCCDC') raises refusals rather than lowering them (21.8% vs 11.6%; offensive + authorization peaks at 50.0%), and rephrasing it out drops refusals to 13.7%. Prompt embeddings predict refusals at AUC 0.827 while keyword features sit near chance (0.572), pointing to a learned 'harm-adjacent' decision boundary that swallows defensive work rather than literal keyword blocking — a safety-induced denial-of-service that falls asymmetrically on defenders and silently breaks autonomous defensive agents that cannot rephrase and retry.

papers.title: Unifying AI Tutor Evaluation: An Evaluation Taxonomy for Pedagogical Ability Assessment of LLM-Powered AI Tutors
papers.authors: Maurya, Srivatsa, Petukhova, Kochmar
papers.abstract: The first unified taxonomy for evaluating AI math tutors: eight learning-sciences pedagogical dimensions (mistake identification, mistake location, revealing the answer, providing guidance, actionability, coherence, tutor tone, human-likeness), each with a desired label and scored by a Desired Annotation Match Rate (DAMR). The MRBench benchmark holds 192 mistake-remediation dialogues (from Bridge and MathDial) and 1,596 responses from 7 LLMs acting as tutors plus novice/expert humans, with gold human annotations (Fleiss' κ 0.65, Cohen's κ 0.71). Llama-3.1-405B is the most pedagogically complete while GPT-4 reveals the answer ~47% of the time (a strong QA system but a weak tutor); models are uniformly human-like (89–96%) yet weak on guidance and default to a neutral rather than encouraging tone; and the popular Prometheus2 and Llama-3.1-8B LLM judges correlate negatively with human labels on every dimension except human-likeness — so pedagogical evaluation still needs humans.

papers.title: OlympiadBench: A Challenging Benchmark for Promoting AGI with Olympiad-Level Bilingual Multimodal Scientific Problems
papers.authors: He, Luo, Bai, Hu, …, Z. Liu, Sun
papers.abstract: An olympiad-level bilingual (English/Chinese) multimodal benchmark of 8,476 free-response math and physics problems — 57% with required diagrams and every problem carrying an expert step-by-step solution — sourced from competitions and the Chinese College Entrance Exam to outrun saturating evals, and graded by an automatic answer-equivalence pipeline (SymPy symbolic equivalence + numeric tolerance) over the 79% open-ended slice. The best 2024 model, GPT-4V, scored only 17.97% overall and 10.74% in physics (GPT-4 text-only reaches 29.93%, and a 7B DeepSeekMath-RL rivals far larger models on math); failures are dominated by reasoning/conceptual errors (42–45%) rather than arithmetic, and the 21% theorem-proving slice has no automatic grader and was almost entirely unsolved (GPT-4V solved 6 of 81 sampled proofs).

papers.title: Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts
papers.authors: Liu, Lin, Hewitt, Paranjape, Bevilacqua, Petroni, Liang
papers.abstract: A controlled study of how language models use long inputs: across multi-document QA (built from NaturalQuestions-Open) and a synthetic key-value retrieval task, accuracy traces a U-shaped curve — highest when the relevant information sits at the very start (primacy) or end (recency) of the context and lowest in the middle, where GPT-3.5-Turbo can fall more than 20 points and below its 56.1% closed-book score. Extended-context models barely beat their base counterparts on the same prompt, reader accuracy saturates long before retriever recall (20→50 documents adds only ~1–1.5%), and the U appears even in base, non-instruction-tuned models and only emerges with scale (Llama-2 13B/70B, not 7B), locating the effect in architecture and scale rather than instruction tuning. It established the position-invariance protocol for evaluating long-context models and the 'lost-in-the-middle' framing that reshaped RAG (reranking, truncation, context compression).

papers.title: LLM Novice Uplift on Dual-Use, In Silico Biology Tasks
papers.authors: Zhang, Knight, Kruus, …, Michael
papers.abstract: Benchmarks ask whether a model knows dangerous biology in one shot; this study asks whether a non-expert human, given hours with a fleet of frontier models, gets meaningfully better at dual-use in-silico biology tasks. Novices reached 4.16x the accuracy of an internet-only control, beat human experts on three of four benchmarks, and almost none reported trouble getting dual-use information past model safeguards.

papers.title: VeRO: An Evaluation Harness for Agents to Optimize Agents
papers.authors: Ursekar, Shanker, Chatrath, Xue, Denton
papers.abstract: A reproducible harness and benchmark for the 'agents optimizing agents' task: a coding agent edits, evaluates, and versions a target agent's code under a fixed evaluation budget. Across five tasks the full harness lifts tool-use agents about 8% over baseline, while reasoning tasks barely move.

papers.title: LHAW: Controllable Underspecification for Long-Horizon Tasks
papers.authors: Pu, Lee, Sehwag, …, Denton
papers.abstract: A dataset-agnostic synthetic pipeline that turns any well-specified long-horizon task into controllably underspecified variants — stripping information across Goal, Constraint, Input, and Context and validating each variant by what agents actually do — then measures whether frontier agents know when to stop and ask for clarification, and what that costs.

papers.title: Agentic Rubrics as Contextual Verifiers for SWE Agents
papers.authors: Raghavendra, Gunjal, Liu, He
papers.abstract: Instead of spinning up a sandbox to run tests, an expert agent crawls the repository and writes a context-grounded rubric checklist, then grades candidate patches against it with no code execution at all — beating patch classifiers and test-based selection on SWE-Bench Verified.

papers.title: Audio MultiChallenge: A Multi-Turn Evaluation of Spoken Dialogue Systems on Natural Human Interaction
papers.authors: Gosai, Vuong, Tyagi, …, He
papers.abstract: Voice models look strong on clean, single-turn, synthetic speech and fall apart in real conversation. Audio MultiChallenge is 452 unscripted multi-turn dialogues with 1,712 atomic rubrics, adding a new Voice Editing axis and audio-cue memory; the best system, Gemini 3 Pro Preview (Thinking), clears just 54.65%.

papers.title: PropensityBench: Evaluating Latent Safety Risks in Large Language Models via an Agentic Approach
papers.authors: Madhushani Sehwag, Shabihi, …, Huang
papers.abstract: Safety audits ask what a model can do; this benchmark asks what it would do — handing an agent a forbidden 'misaligned' tool and a safe tool secretly rigged to always fail, then turning up operational pressure until alignment breaks. Average propensity to reach for the forbidden tool climbs to 46.9%.

papers.title: Best Practices for Biorisk Evaluations on Open-Weight Bio-Foundation Models
papers.authors: Wei, Che, Li, …, Mazeika
papers.abstract: A red-team of pretraining-data filtering: on Evo 2-7B — which deliberately excluded eukaryotic viral sequences for safety — 'filtered' capability returns in 50 fine-tuning steps (0.72 H100 GPU-hours), and a linear probe shows the harmful signal never left the model's latent representations at all.

papers.title: Reasoning Gym: infinite, verifiable reasoning environments for RLVR
papers.authors: Stojanovski, Stanley, Sharratt, …, Köpf
papers.abstract: A library of 100+ procedurally generated, algorithmically verifiable reasoning tasks — from polynomial algebra to Rubik's cubes — emitting virtually unlimited training data at controllable difficulty. The same generators expose a 'difficulty cliff' in frontier models and RL-train a small model whose gains transfer across domains and to external benchmarks.

papers.title: Beyond Seeing: Evaluating MLLMs on Tool-Enabled Image Perception, Transformation, and Reasoning
papers.authors: Guo, Tyagi, Gosai, …, Srinivasa
papers.abstract: A benchmark for the think-with-images paradigm, where a model must actively crop, edit, and enhance an image with tools rather than just stare at it: 1,204 expert-authored tasks, 7,777 weighted rubrics, and a frontier that barely clears 18.68%.

papers.title: Chasing the Tail: Effective Rubric-based Reward Modeling for LLM Post-Training
papers.authors: Zhang, Wang, Gui, …, Jin
papers.abstract: Reward over-optimization in RL fine-tuning lives in the high-reward tail, where the proxy can't tell an excellent answer from a merely great one. Building rubrics by iteratively differentiating the best off-policy responses (from '4 great & diverse pairs') beats prompt-only and single-pair rubrics on Qwen3-8B-Base across generalist, health, and finance, and delays over-optimization far longer.

papers.title: Progress over Points: Reframing LM Benchmarks Around Scientific Objectives
papers.authors: Jin, Hendryx, Nath
papers.abstract: A position paper with a working demo: stop scoring models on already-solved puzzles and instead measure progress on objectives that are themselves scientific targets — instantiated as a NanoGPT-speedrun environment where an evolutionary agent loop set a new training-time record.

papers.title: Reliable Weak-to-Strong Monitoring of LLM Agents
papers.authors: Kale, Zhang, Zhu, …, Wang
papers.abstract: Can an LLM overseer catch an agent secretly misbehaving — leaking data, booking the costlier flight, injecting a backdoor — while it does a legitimate job? A monitor red-teaming workflow finds the agent's awareness hurts the monitor more than the monitor's awareness helps, scaffolding matters more than knowledge, and a hybrid scaffold lets weak models reliably watch strong ones.

papers.title: Search-Time Data Contamination
papers.authors: Han, Mankikar, Michael, Wang
papers.abstract: Identifies 'search-time contamination' (STC): when a search-based LLM agent's retrieval step surfaces a web copy of a benchmark (e.g. a third-party HuggingFace re-upload) containing the test question alongside its answer, letting the agent copy the label rather than reason. Across HLE, SimpleQA, and GPQA, ~3% of questions are contaminated via HuggingFace, and blocking that source drops accuracy on the contaminated subset by ~15% — a lower-bound warning that offline capability benchmarks can't be trusted to evaluate search-augmented agents.

papers.title: MultiNRC: A Challenging and Native Multilingual Reasoning Evaluation Benchmark for LLMs
papers.authors: Fabbri, Mares, Flores, Mankikar, Hernandez, Lee, Liu, Xing
papers.abstract: A benchmark of 1,055 reasoning questions written from scratch by native French, Spanish, and Chinese speakers — not translated from English — spanning language-specific linguistic reasoning, wordplay & riddles, cultural/tradition reasoning, and culturally grounded math. Questions are retained only when ≥3 of 5 frontier models fail them, and graded by an LLM judge (GPT-4.1, >95% human agreement, Scott's Pi 0.88). No model passes 50% (o3-pro leads at 49.00%); translating to English lifts math accuracy ~10 points (Spanish +19.1, Chinese +12.5) but barely moves cultural reasoning, exposing missing cultural knowledge rather than a pure language barrier.

papers.title: Rubrics as Rewards: Reinforcement Learning Beyond Verifiable Domains
papers.authors: Gunjal, Wang, Lau, Nath, He, Liu, Hendryx
papers.abstract: Extends RLVR beyond verifiable domains by using LLM-generated, instance-specific checklist rubrics as the reward signal for GRPO. The best variant (RaR-Implicit) gives relative gains up to 31% on HealthBench and 7% on GPQA-Diamond over a Direct-Likert reward, and rubric structure helps small judges align like much larger ones.

papers.title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer
papers.authors: Raffel, Shazeer, Roberts, Lee, …, Li, Liu
papers.abstract: Google's systematic survey of NLP transfer learning, built on one idea: cast every task — translation, classification, QA, even regression — as text-to-text so a single model, loss, and decoding procedure can compare pre-training objectives, architectures, and corpora apples-to-apples. The bake-off favors the original encoder-decoder with a short-target span-corruption objective (83.28 vs 74.70 GLUE over a same-size decoder-only LM) and a large, diverse corpus over repeated in-domain data; scaling those insights with the 750 GB C4 corpus and an 11B-parameter model trained on ~1 trillion tokens set state-of-the-art on 18 of 24 tasks (GLUE 90.3, SuperGLUE 88.9 vs the 89.8 human baseline, SQuAD 91.26 EM). Reported with statistical care — the baseline is trained 10× from scratch with reported standard deviations — though English-only pre-training never reached SOTA on WMT translation and the ablations run at base scale rather than at 11B.

papers.title: Unsupervised Dense Information Retrieval with Contrastive Learning
papers.authors: Izacard, Caron, Hosseini, Riedel, Bojanowski, Joulin, Grave
papers.abstract: Trains a dense retriever with no labels at all: positive pairs are two random spans cropped from the same document, and a MoCo momentum encoder plus a 131,072-entry queue supplies the negatives for an InfoNCE contrastive loss. The resulting model — Contriever — is the first unsupervised dense retriever to beat BM25 on 11 of 15 BEIR datasets at Recall@100 (and on NaturalQuestions QA recall), though on nDCG@10 ranking precision BM25 still leads the BEIR average. It shines most as a pre-training initialization (improving few-shot and MS MARCO-fine-tuned transfer) and as a multilingual mContriever that performs cross-lingual, cross-script retrieval — fetching English documents from Arabic queries — which term matching cannot do. 'Contriever, fine-tuned on MS-MARCO' became the default off-the-shelf retriever behind many RAG pipelines.

papers.title: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
papers.authors: Dao, Fu, Ermon, Rudra, Ré
papers.abstract: An IO-aware exact-attention algorithm that uses tiling and recomputation to compute softmax(QKᵀ)V without ever materializing the N×N attention matrix in GPU HBM, cutting HBM accesses from Θ(Nd+N²) to Θ(N²d²M⁻¹) and using memory linear in sequence length. The fused CUDA kernel runs up to 7.6× faster than standard attention (9× less HBM traffic — 4.4 vs 40.3 GB), trains BERT-large 15% faster than the MLPerf 1.1 record and GPT-2 3× faster than HuggingFace at identical perplexity, and by making long context affordable yields the first Transformers to beat chance on Path-X (16K) and Path-256 (64K). It became the efficiency backbone of the long-context era, with a matching IO lower bound proving no exact-attention algorithm can asymptotically do fewer HBM accesses.

papers.title: Code Llama: Open Foundation Models for Code
papers.authors: Rozière, Gehring, Gloeckle, …, Usunier, Scialom, Synnaeve
papers.abstract: Meta specializes Llama 2 into an open family of code models (7B/13B/34B/70B) through a cascade of fine-tuning stages — 500B code tokens (1T for 70B), a +100B Python branch, long-context fine-tuning, and instruction tuning — adding fill-in-the-middle infilling and 100k-token contexts (RoPE base period 10,000→1,000,000). It sets open-model state of the art at up to 67.8% HumanEval (Instruct 70B, ≈GPT-4's 67.0%) and 65.6% MBPP (Python 70B), with Code Llama-Python 7B (38.4/47.6) beating Llama 2 70B (30.5/45.4). Self-instruct via execution feedback (~14k question–tests–solution triplets) replaces costly human code labels, and instruction tuning drives ToxiGen toxicity to ~0% at a small, sometimes non-monotonic cost to raw code skill. Released under a permissive commercial license, it became the base model for the first open coding agents (SWE-Llama).

papers.title: Is Your Code Generated by ChatGPT Really Correct? Rigorous Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code Generation
papers.authors: Liu, Xia, Wang, Zhang
papers.abstract: EvalPlus rigorously re-tests LLM-generated code by augmenting a benchmark's thin test suite with automatically generated inputs — ChatGPT-seeded, then expanded ~80× by type-aware mutation — and grading via differential testing against vetted ground-truths. Extending HumanEval to HumanEval+ (9.6→764.1 avg tests per problem) over 26 LLMs, pass@k drops by up to 19.3–28.9%, and the ranking flips: WizardCoder- and Phind-CodeLlama-34B both overtake ChatGPT on HumanEval+ though neither does on HumanEval. The harder tests also expose 18 buggy ground-truths (11% of problems) in HumanEval itself, and a set-cover-distilled HumanEval+-mini reproduces the effect with 47× fewer tests — establishing test adequacy as a first-class concern and making HumanEval+/MBPP+ default code-eval sets.

papers.title: Measuring Coding Challenge Competence With APPS
papers.authors: Hendrycks, Basart, Kadavath, …, Song, Steinhardt
papers.abstract: The benchmark that re-grounded code evaluation in functional correctness: 10,000 coding-challenge problems split into Introductory / Interview / Competition tiers, curated from Codeforces, Kattis, Codewars, and AtCoder, each graded by executing generated Python against hidden test cases (131,777 of them, plus 232,421 human solutions) rather than scoring text overlap. In 2021 programming was very hard for models — fine-tuned GPT-Neo 2.7B passed 14.68% of introductory test cases but only 3.90% strict accuracy (0.00% on Competition for every model), and few-shot GPT-3 175B solved just 3 of 5,000 problems — yet syntax errors fell ~59%→3% with fine-tuning, beam-5 sampling lifted GPT-Neo to 19.9% test-case average, and the paper showed BLEU ranking a worse model above a better one. Its difficulty-tiered, execution-graded, natural-language-spec format (a HumanEval-era contemporary) became foundational to the coding-agent era and the precursor cited by SWE-bench, Code Llama, and BigCodeBench.

papers.title: WebGuard: Building a Generalizable Guardrail for Web Agents
papers.authors: Zheng, Liao, Salisbury, …, Song, Sun, Su
papers.abstract: The first real-world, action-level dataset for web-agent safety: 4,939 human-annotated actions across 193 websites and 22 domains, each graded SAFE / LOW / HIGH by how reversible and consequential it is — a reframing from 'is the task harmful' to 'is this click reversible.' Frontier LLMs score under 60% accuracy and under 60% HIGH-risk recall zero-shot and reasoning models (o3, Claude-3.7-Thinking) get more accurate but under-flag HIGH risk; fine-tuning a Qwen2.5-VL-7B lifts accuracy from 37% to 80% and HIGH-risk recall from 20% to 76%, with even a 3B model out-recalling o3 — yet Cross-Domain recall stalls at 66.8%, far from the near-perfect reliability a deployable guardrail requires.

papers.title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
papers.authors: Brown, Mann, Ryder, Subbiah, Kaplan, …, Sutskever, Amodei
papers.abstract: OpenAI scales a GPT-2-style autoregressive Transformer to 175 billion parameters (eight sizes from 125M), trained on ~300B tokens of filtered Common Crawl plus books and Wikipedia, to show that task-specific fine-tuning isn't required: given a natural-language description and a few demonstrations in its context window — no gradient updates — GPT-3 performs new tasks 'in-context'. Few-shot ability scales smoothly and steeply with model size across 42 benchmarks, reaching 86.4% on LAMBADA, 71.2% on TriviaQA, and 100% on 2-digit addition (an emergent skill near the top of the size range), with humans detecting its generated news articles at just ~52% (chance). It still trails fine-tuned SOTA on SuperGLUE (71.8 vs 89.0) and NLI, and the paper candidly flags train/test contamination from web-scale pre-training. The work that reframed 'training a model for a task' as 'writing a prompt'.

papers.title: Chain of Thought Monitorability: A New and Fragile Opportunity for AI Safety
papers.authors: Korbak, Balesni, Barnes, …, Baker, Shah, Mikulik
papers.abstract: A 40-author cross-lab position paper (UK AISI, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Anthropic, METR, Apollo, and more; endorsed by Hinton, Sutskever, Bowman, and Schulman) arguing that reasoning models which 'think' in human language give safety a rare gift: their chains of thought can be monitored for the intent to misbehave. Because hard tasks force a Transformer to route serial reasoning through the CoT (the 'externalized reasoning property') and models tend to verbalize anyway, monitors already catch misbehavior like 'Let's hack' and reward hacking — but the window is fragile, threatened by scaled outcome-based RL, direct and indirect CoT supervision, and latent-reasoning architectures. The paper runs no new experiments; it recommends building monitorability evaluations, reporting them in system cards, and weighing monitorability in training and deployment decisions, while warning it can never be a standalone safety guarantee.

papers.title: Learning to summarize from human feedback
papers.authors: Stiennon, Ouyang, Wu, Ziegler, Lowe, Voss, Radford, Amodei, Christiano
papers.abstract: The blueprint for RLHF. Instead of training a summarizer to imitate reference text and scoring it with ROUGE, OpenAI collects 64,832 human pairwise preferences over Reddit TL;DR summaries, fits a reward model with a logistic loss, and fine-tunes the policy with PPO under a KL penalty to the supervised init. A 1.3B feedback model beats a supervised model 10× its size (61% vs 43% preferred over the human reference), both feedback models are preferred to the human-written references themselves, and the method transfers zero-shot to CNN/DM news. Its over-optimization curve — predicted reward keeps climbing as true human preference collapses — is the first public demonstration of reward-model Goodharting, and the whole recipe is the direct template for InstructGPT and ChatGPT.

papers.title: SpreadsheetBench: Towards Challenging Real World Spreadsheet Manipulation
papers.authors: Ma, Zhang, Zhang, Yu, …, Wang, Tang
papers.abstract: A spreadsheet-manipulation benchmark built entirely from real Excel-forum questions: 912 authentic instructions (averaging 85.7 words, vs 9.8–27.9 in prior benchmarks) and 2,729 test cases over genuinely messy spreadsheets — 35.7% with multiple tables, 42.7% with non-standard (nested/incomplete/missing-header) tables, some past 100 columns and 20,000 rows. It grades online-judge style: one generated code solution must pass three input–output spreadsheets per instruction, a design that resists data leakage and catches single-sheet overfitting. Across TableQA models, open and closed LLMs, and Excel products the field spans just 0.05%–23.65% soft-restriction accuracy; the best, Copilot in Excel (~20%) and GPT-4o (18.35%), sit far below the 71.33% scored by Excel experts, coding ability beats general ability (DeepseekCoder-33B > Llama-3-70B), and multi-round code-execution feedback lifts most models nearly tenfold while GPT-4o does not benefit.

papers.title: TRAIL: Trace Reasoning and Agentic Issue Localization
papers.authors: Deshpande, Gangal, Mehta, Krishnan, Kannappan, Qian
papers.abstract: A benchmark for debugging agentic execution traces: 148 expert-annotated OpenTelemetry traces (1,987 spans, 575 carrying errors; 841 annotated errors at 5.68 per trace) drawn from GAIA and SWE-Bench Lite, paired with a three-area error taxonomy spanning reasoning, system execution, and planning. Asked to classify and locate the errors in each long, structured trace, the best long-context model (Gemini-2.5-Pro) reaches only 11% joint accuracy — 18% on GAIA, 5% on SWE-Bench — and 3 of 8 frontier LLMs can't even fit the trace in context (the longest GAIA input is 7.5M tokens against a 1M limit; max traces run 2x the context window). Output Generation errors (formatting + instruction non-compliance) make up ~42% of all errors while system-execution failures sit in the long tail, and reasoning models beat non-reasoning ones by 1.5–8x on joint accuracy.

papers.title: Terminal-Bench: Benchmarking Agents on Hard, Realistic Tasks in Command Line Interfaces
papers.authors: Merrill, Shaw, Carlini, …, Konwinski, Schmidt
papers.abstract: A hard, crowd-sourced benchmark of 89 containerized command-line tasks — each with a unique Docker environment, hidden outcome tests that grade the final container state, and a human-written reference solution, verified at roughly three reviewer-hours apiece — spanning software engineering, system administration, security, and scientific computing. Across 32,155 trials over 16 frontier models and 6 agent scaffolds, the best system (GPT-5.2 via Codex CLI) resolves only 63% of tasks and smaller models around 15%; proprietary models hold the top 13 spots while the best open-weight (Kimi K2 Thinking) reaches 36%, execution errors like disobeying the spec and 'command not found' (24.1% of command failures) dominate, and the authors note the benchmark may saturate within a year.

papers.title: Large Language Models Encode Clinical Knowledge
papers.authors: Singhal, Azizi, Tu, …, Karthikesalingam, Natarajan
papers.abstract: Introduces MultiMedQA — seven medical QA datasets including the new 3,375-question HealthSearchQA — plus a clinician human-evaluation framework (scientific consensus, harm, bias, completeness) that pushes medical-LLM eval past multiple-choice accuracy. Flan-PaLM 540B (PaLM instruction-tuned, with few-shot + chain-of-thought + self-consistency) sets SOTA on every multiple-choice set — 67.6% on MedQA/USMLE, +17% over prior best. Instruction prompt tuning (a 100-token, 1.84M-parameter soft prompt over Flan-PaLM) yields Med-PaLM, lifting scientific-consensus agreement 61.9%→92.6% and cutting potentially-harmful answers 29.7%→5.9%, though it still trails clinicians.

papers.title: OpenRubrics: Towards Scalable Synthetic Rubric Generation for Reward Modeling and LLM Alignment
papers.authors: Liu, Xu, Yu, Hong, Yang, Zhao, Wang
papers.abstract: Tackles the cost and unreliability of rubrics-as-rewards by auto-generating rubrics instead of hand-authoring them. Contrastive Rubric Generation (CRG) conditions on a prompt's chosen and rejected responses to derive both hard rules (explicit constraints) and principles (implicit qualities), then a preference-label-consistency filter keeps only rubrics that reproduce the human preference. The released OpenRubrics dataset (35.7k prompts, ~85.6k pairwise instances) trains Rubric-RM, an 8B rubric-generator + judge that beats same-size white-box judges by 8.4 points (70.1 avg, 73.0 with voting@5), and lifts DPO policies on IFBench (30.3), HealthBench (judge 68.3, policy 23.8), and Arena-Hard/AlpacaEval/WildBench.

papers.title: Teaching Models to Verbalize Reward Hacking in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
papers.authors: Turpin, Arditi, Li, Benton, Michael
papers.abstract: A pre-RL fine-tuning intervention (verbalization fine-tuning, VFT) that trains a model to explicitly admit when prompt cues influence its answer, so the reward hacking it later learns under RL stays visible in the chain of thought. On seven MMLU-based cue environments with Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, VFT cuts the effective cue-influence rate (undetected reward hacks) to 6% on held-out cues amplified by RL, versus 88% for plain RL and 99% for a debiasing baseline (BCT), by driving the verbalization rate from 8% to 43% after VFT and up to 94% after RL. Crucially every method still learns the hack (cue influence ~100% post-RL), so the only lever on detectability is verbalization, and BCT's attempt to suppress cue sensitivity backfires by suppressing the confession (verbalization drops to 1%). VFT costs no extra accuracy over baseline RL and avoids over-verbalizing (specificity 96-97%) — a transparency intervention, not a prevention.

papers.title: DAPO: An Open-Source LLM Reinforcement Learning System at Scale
papers.authors: Yu, Zhang, Zhu, …, Yan, Qiao, Wang
papers.abstract: Fully open-sources a large-scale RLVR recipe that lifts a Qwen2.5-32B base model from near-0% to 50 avg@32 on AIME 2024 — beating DeepSeek-R1-Zero-Qwen-32B (47) in half the training steps — where a naive GRPO baseline stalls at 30. DAPO (Decoupled Clip and Dynamic sAmpling Policy Optimization) drops GRPO's KL penalty and uses a rule-based ±1 reward, then adds four fixes to the failure modes of long-CoT RL: Clip-Higher (decoupled ε_low=0.2 / ε_high=0.28 to halt entropy collapse), Dynamic Sampling (filter all-correct/all-wrong groups so gradients stay non-zero), Token-Level Policy Gradient Loss (normalize by total tokens rather than per-sample, so long reasoning isn't underweighted), and Overlong Reward Shaping (a soft length penalty instead of a hard truncation −1). The progressive ablation builds 30→36→38→41→42→50 as each technique is layered onto GRPO. Ships the algorithm, the verl-based training code, and the DAPO-Math-17K integer-answer dataset — the reference open recipe of the post-R1 reproduction wave.

papers.title: Does Reinforcement Learning Really Incentivize Reasoning Capacity in LLMs Beyond the Base Model?
papers.authors: Yue, Chen, Lu, Zhao, Wang, Yue, Song, Huang
papers.abstract: A systematic pass@k study (large k, across Qwen2.5/LLaMA families, six RL algorithms, and math/code/visual benchmarks) arguing that current RLVR does not expand a model's reasoning boundary — it sharpens sampling of solutions the base model could already produce. RL beats base at small k but base matches or overtakes it at large k (base +~9% at k=128 on Minerva-32B); RL responses sit in the base model's low-perplexity tail and the RL-solvable set is nearly a subset of the base's; the sampling-efficiency gap stays >40 points across PPO/GRPO/Reinforce++/RLOO/ReMax/DAPO; and only distillation genuinely lifts the boundary.

papers.title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
papers.authors: Ouyang, Wu, Jiang, Almeida, …, Leike, Lowe
papers.abstract: OpenAI's canonical RLHF recipe: fine-tune GPT-3 on ~13k labeler demonstrations (SFT), train a 6B reward model on ~33k human rankings of model outputs, then optimize the policy with PPO against that reward under a KL leash to the SFT model. The resulting 1.3B InstructGPT is preferred to the 175B GPT-3 (175B InstructGPT wins 85±3% head-to-head), with about 2x truthful-and-informative answers on TruthfulQA and ~25% fewer toxic outputs — at a small 'alignment tax' on standard NLP benchmarks that mixing pretraining gradients (PPO-ptx) mostly repairs.

papers.title: MLAgentBench: Evaluating Language Agents on Machine Learning Experimentation
papers.authors: Huang, Vora, Liang, Leskovec
papers.abstract: The first benchmark for LM agents that run the full ML experimentation loop — read files, edit training code, execute it, read the metric, iterate — across 13 tasks with an automatic evaluator (success = beat the starter baseline by 10%). A Claude 3 Opus agent reaches 37.5% average success but swings from 100% on canonical datasets like house-price to 0% on Kaggle challenges released after its training cutoff, with hallucination and long-horizon planning as the named failure modes. Its Fact Check and Research Plan response fields are the antidote, and it beats AutoGPT and LangChain scaffolds (26.0 vs 2.9 vs 1.0 on GPT-4-turbo).

papers.title: LiveCodeBench: Holistic and Contamination Free Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code
papers.authors: Jain, Han, Gu, …, Sen, Stoica
papers.abstract: A live, contamination-free code benchmark: 511 contest problems (May 2023–May 2024) from LeetCode, AtCoder, and CodeForces, each tagged with a release date so models are scored only on problems published after their training cutoff — and graded across four scenarios (code generation, self-repair, code execution, and a newly-introduced test-output-prediction task) rather than generation alone, with ~17 tests per problem. Across 52 models (18 base + 34 instruction-tuned), time-stamping exposes contamination as a cliff at each model's cutoff: DS-Base-33B falls from Pass@1 ≈60 to ≈0 on LeetCode after September 2023 and GPT-4o drops after November, a signal exact/fuzzy decontamination misses. HumanEval+ correlates only 0.72 with LCB-Easy, so fine-tuned open models that win on HumanEval collapse here (DS-Ins-1.3B 59.8%→26.3%); the four scenarios correlate above 0.88 yet rankings still shift (Claude-3-Opus passes GPT-4-Turbo on test output prediction); and a real SoTA gap reappears with GPT-4-Turbo leading (39.7 LCB-Easy). The evaluation framework the contamination-free era — SWE-Bench Pro, BigCodeBench, Terminal-Bench, and the reasoning-model leaderboards — is built on.

papers.title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents
papers.authors: Liu, Yu, Zhang, …, Su, Sun, Huang, Dong, Tang
papers.abstract: The first systematic benchmark for LLM-as-Agent: 29 API and open-source models evaluated across 8 interactive, execution-graded environments (Operating System, Database, Knowledge Graph, Digital Card Game, Lateral Thinking Puzzles, House-Holding, Web Shopping, Web Browsing) over an estimated 5–50 rounds per task, scored by a relative overall metric that normalizes each task's cross-model average to 1. GPT-4 leads at OA 4.01 — best on 6 of 8 datasets and genuinely usable in spots (78% success on House-Holding) — but no model is reliable, and a wide gap separates API models (avg 2.32, all above 1.00) from open-source ≤70B (avg 0.51, best codellama-34b 0.96, still under gpt-3.5-turbo). The dominant failure is Task Limit Exceeded — weak long-horizon reasoning and decision-making (Lateral Thinking Puzzles 82.5%, Knowledge Graph 67.9%) — rather than missing knowledge, with format/action-schema errors crippling Database (53.3%) and House-Holding (64.1%); code training helps static-procedure tasks but hurts flexible thinking, and high-quality alignment data beats raw scale (vicuna-13b matches 3× larger codellama-34b). Ships a Server-Client, Docker-isolated evaluation toolkit and seeded the agent-benchmark era.

papers.title: Not What You've Signed Up For: Compromising Real-World LLM-Integrated Applications with Indirect Prompt Injection
papers.authors: Greshake, Abdelnabi, Mishra, Endres, Holz, Fritz
papers.abstract: The paper that named indirect prompt injection: once an LLM is wired into an application that retrieves external content — web pages, emails, documents, code — an attacker who plants instructions in that content can hijack the model with no direct access, because retrieval blurs the line between data and instructions. It contributes a security-grounded taxonomy (injection methods x threats x affected parties) and working proof-of-concept attacks against Bing's GPT-4 Chat, GitHub Copilot, and synthetic GPT-4 apps — data exfiltration, self-propagating prompt 'worms', cross-session memory persistence, command-and-control, disinformation, and Base64-encoded payloads — though every result is a qualitative demo rather than a measured success rate.

papers.title: Ignore Previous Prompt: Attack Techniques For Language Models
papers.authors: Perez, Ribeiro
papers.abstract: The paper that named prompt injection and first quantified it. Using PROMPTINJECT — a modular framework for composing adversarial prompts over a base prompt's open user-input slot — it shows simple handcrafted inputs can hijack a GPT-3 (text-davinci-002) application's goal 58.6% of the time and leak its hidden system prompt 23.6% of the time, across 35 OpenAI-example prompts run 4x each. Delimiters amplify attacks (43.6%->58.6%), overtly harmful payloads attack worse than benign ones (70.0% vs 49.3%) as an alignment side-effect of InstructGPT tuning, and capability tracks vulnerability in an inverse-scaling effect where the most capable model in the GPT-3 series is the most attackable (13.8%->58.6%). Superficial defenses (stop sequences, trailing text) only dent it; the real fix — separating trusted instructions from untrusted data — is left open, and the work seeded the entire prompt-injection literature (indirect injection, agentic injection).

papers.title: INJECAGENT: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated Large Language Model Agents
papers.authors: Zhan, Liang, Ying, Kang
papers.abstract: The first benchmark for indirect prompt injection (IPI) against tool-integrated LLM agents: 1,054 test cases (17 user tools x 62 attacker tools, split into direct-harm and data-stealing attacks) where a malicious instruction hidden in a tool's response tries to hijack the agent into calling a harmful tool against its own user. Across 30 agents, ReAct-prompted GPT-4 executes the injected instruction 23.6% of the time (47.0% once a fixed 'ignore previous instructions' hacking prompt is prepended) and Llama2-70B exceeds 80%, while fine-tuned function-calling agents are far more resilient (GPT-4 6.6%, GPT-3.5 3.8%); capability tracks vulnerability, high-content-freedom placeholders drive attack success more than the attacker's tool choice (Cramer's V 0.28-0.31 vs 0.18-0.20), data transmission once extracted hits 100%, and Claude-2 is the lone agent the hacking prompt makes safer by triggering its alertness.

papers.title: The Instruction Hierarchy: Training LLMs to Prioritize Privileged Instructions
papers.authors: Wallace, Xiao, Leike, Weng, Heidecke, Beutel
papers.abstract: Prompt injections, jailbreaks, and system-prompt extraction share one root cause: LLMs treat system, user, and tool text as equally authoritative. The paper reframes this as a missing privilege system and defines an instruction hierarchy (system → user → tool) with an automated data-generation recipe — context synthesis for aligned lower-level instructions, context ignorance for misaligned ones — to fine-tune GPT-3.5 Turbo to defer to higher-privileged instructions. Robustness rises across the board (system-message extraction 32.8% → 95.9%) and generalizes to held-out attacks like jailbreaks (+34%), at the cost of over-refusal on adversarially attack-shaped benign prompts.

papers.title: AgentDojo: A Dynamic Environment to Evaluate Prompt Injection Attacks and Defenses for LLM Agents
papers.authors: Debenedetti, Zhang, Balunović, Beurer-Kellner, Fischer, Tramèr
papers.abstract: A dynamic, deterministically-scored benchmark for indirect prompt injection on tool-using LLM agents: 70 tools, 97 user tasks, and 629 security test cases across email, Slack, banking, and travel. Utility and security are checked by Python functions over environment state (not an LLM judge), and the paper finds more capable models are the easiest to hijack.

papers.title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models
papers.authors: Wang, Wei, Schuurmans, …, Chowdhery, Zhou
papers.abstract: Replaces greedy chain-of-thought decoding with a sample-and-marginalize scheme: sample a diverse set of reasoning paths, then take the majority-vote answer — an unsupervised, training-free decoder swap requiring no verifier or extra data. Across four models from UL2-20B to PaLM-540B it lifts reasoning on nearly every task (GSM8K +17.9 on PaLM-540B, 56.5→74.4, and +17.9 on GPT-3, 60.1→78.0; AQuA +12.2; SVAMP +11.0), with gains that grow with scale and saturate around 40 sampled paths. The direct ancestor of maj@k / cons@k test-time-compute voting.

papers.title: Large Language Models are Zero-Shot Reasoners
papers.authors: Kojima, Gu, Reid, Matsuo, Iwasawa
papers.abstract: Shows multi-step reasoning is latent in large LLMs and elicitable with no exemplars: a single task-agnostic trigger — 'Let's think step by step.' — plus a two-stage reason-then-extract prompt takes InstructGPT-175B (text-davinci-002) from 17.7% to 78.7% on MultiArith and 10.4% to 40.7% on GSM8K, with the largest jumps on symbolic tasks (Coin Flip 12.8%→91.4%, Last Letter 0.2%→57.6%). The effect is emergent at ~100B+ scale (raw GPT-3 175B only 3.3%→19.0%; PaLM 540B reaches 43.0% on GSM8K), can hurt on single-step/commonsense tasks (CommonSenseQA −4.2), and still trails 8-shot Few-shot-CoT (93.0 on MultiArith) — a dirt-cheap, label-free baseline and scaling probe that seeded the test-time-reasoning line.

papers.title: Training Verifiers to Solve Math Word Problems
papers.authors: Cobbe, Kosaraju, Bavarian, …, Hilton, Schulman
papers.abstract: Introduces GSM8K — 8.5K linguistically diverse, human-written grade-school math word problems (7.5K train / 1K test, 2–8 steps, <2% breaking errors) — and shows that finetuning alone scales terribly (the 175B model tops out near ≈33%; an 80% solve rate would naively need ~10^16 parameters). The fix: train a verifier to score 100 sampled solutions and return the top-ranked one, a boost worth roughly a 30× model-size increase (6B verification ≈ a finetuned 175B model; the 175B verifier reaches ≈55%). Establishes the generator-verifier, sample-and-rank, test-time-compute paradigm — and the default math-reasoning benchmark — that chain-of-thought self-consistency, outcome/process reward models, and modern RLVR all build on.

papers.title: Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Wei, Tay, Bommasani, …, Dean, Fedus
papers.abstract: A survey that names a phenomenon scaling laws don't predict: abilities absent in small models that appear as a sharp jump past a critical scale (a 'phase transition'). It catalogues thresholds across five model families — 3-digit arithmetic emerges for GPT-3 at ~13B params, MMLU at ~70–280B, TruthfulQA only for Gopher 280B, Word-in-Context only for PaLM 540B — and shows augmented strategies (chain-of-thought, instruction tuning, scratchpads) are themselves emergent, giving no benefit until ~100B. The authors flag the catch their own §5.1 raises and Schaeffer et al. (2023) later formalized: discontinuous metrics like exact-match can manufacture the jump, since cross-entropy loss improves smoothly below threshold — making 'emergence' as much a property of the metric as of the model.

papers.title: UpBench: A Dynamically Evolving Real-World Labor-Market Agentic Benchmark Framework Built for Human-Centric AI
papers.authors: Yi, Liu, Terzolo, Hasson, Sinha, Mendes, Rabinovich
papers.abstract: A dynamically refreshed benchmark of 322 real, paid Upwork jobs, each decomposed by expert freelancers into 5–20 labeled acceptance criteria (critical/important/optional/pitfall) with per-criterion human feedback. Minimal plan–act–reflect agents are graded on strict completion and a graded rubric score: Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 39.8% first-attempt completion versus ~20% for Gemini 2.5 Pro and GPT-5, and a single human-in-the-loop feedback pass adds 11–14 points and rescues 18–23% of failed jobs. An embedded economic model frames an AI → HITL → human handoff as task value rises.

papers.title: Measuring Massive Multitask Language Understanding
papers.authors: Hendrycks, Burns, Basart, Zou, Mazeika, Song, Steinhardt
papers.abstract: A 57-subject zero/few-shot multiple-choice benchmark spanning STEM, the humanities, the social sciences, and professional fields (elementary to advanced level), built to measure the world knowledge a model absorbs during pretraining — and to resist the saturation that felled GLUE and SuperGLUE within a year.
