In 2020 few-shot GPT-3 up to 13B sat at random chance (~25%) and only the 175B model reached 43.9% average — while a fine-tuned 11B UnifiedQA actually led at 48.9% under distribution shift — with performance lopsided (near-chance on Professional Law and Moral Scenarios despite ~70% on its best subject) and badly miscalibrated, its confidence off from accuracy by up to 24%. The 4-option-MCQ, single-average format that made it cheap, comparable, and durable also became its limit, and MMLU became the canonical LLM knowledge benchmark that MMLU-Pro, GPQA, and BIG-Bench were later built to extend or escape.

papers.title: Measuring Mathematical Problem Solving With the MATH Dataset
papers.authors: Hendrycks, Burns, Kadavath, …, Song, Steinhardt
papers.abstract: A benchmark of 12,500 competition-mathematics problems (AMC/AIME), each with a full worked solution and a single boxed answer graded by exact match. In 2021 the best models scored 3–7%, and a log-linear extrapolation suggested scaling alone would need ~10^35 parameters to reach 40% — a forecast chain-of-thought, scale, and RL later overturned. It also introduced AMPS, a 5M-problem pretraining corpus.

papers.title: TruthfulQA: Measuring How Models Mimic Human Falsehoods
papers.authors: Lin, Hilton, Evans
papers.abstract: An 817-question benchmark across 38 categories engineered to elicit imitative falsehoods — false answers that score high likelihood because humans repeat them (e.g. that coughing stops a heart attack). The best 2021 model (GPT-3-175B, hand-picked 'helpful' prompt) is truthful on just 58% of questions vs 94% for a human, and is confidently false-and-informative 42% of the time vs 6% for the human. The signature result is inverse scaling: within every family the larger model is the less truthful one (largest GPT-Neo/J 17% worse than a 60x-smaller one), while a control set of matched trivia scales normally — evidence the failures are imitative, not surface-form. A finetuned GPT-judge (GPT-3-6.7B) predicts human truth labels at 90–96% accuracy. The honesty benchmark that reframed truthfulness as measurable and argued fine-tuning/RLHF, not scale, is the path to truthful models.

papers.title: GPQA: A Graduate-Level Google-Proof Q&A Benchmark
papers.authors: Rein, Hou, Cooper Stickland, Petty, Pang, Dirani, Michael, Bowman
papers.abstract: A scalable-oversight testbed rather than a leaderboard: 448 graduate-level multiple-choice questions in biology, physics, and chemistry, written and validated by 61 PhD contractors through a four-stage pipeline so the answers are objective for in-domain experts (~65% accuracy, ~74% estimated objectivity) yet 'Google-proof' for skilled non-experts, who reach only 34.1% despite a median 30 minutes and unrestricted web access (25% is chance). At release the strongest baseline, GPT-4 with a search tool, managed just 39.4% on the extended set (and abstained on 37.2% of main-set questions), far below experts and barely above non-experts. Three nested splits — Extended (546), main (448), and the strictest GPQA Diamond (198, both experts correct and a majority of non-experts wrong) — with Diamond now the canonical hard-science subset reported in frontier model cards. Validator accuracies on the main/diamond subsets are skewed by the selection that defines them.

papers.title: Beyond the Imitation Game: Quantifying and Extrapolating the Capabilities of Language Models
papers.authors: Srivastava, Rastogi, Rao, … (450 authors)
papers.abstract: An open, collaboratively built benchmark of 204 tasks from 450 authors across 132 institutions, deliberately stocked with problems believed to be beyond current models and spanning linguistics, math, common-sense reasoning, biology, software, and social bias. Evaluating OpenAI GPT, Google dense (BIG-G) and Switch-style sparse transformers, and PaLM from millions to hundreds of billions of parameters against an expert-human baseline, it finds the best models score under 20 on a 0–100 human-normalized scale (experts ≈100); calibration improves with scale but stays poor (Brier 0.2–0.3, ECE 0.25–0.45); per-token cross-entropy scales lawfully (exponent ≈ −0.066 to −0.069) while downstream capability does not; tasks split into gradual 'linear' (knowledge/memorization) versus sudden 'breakthrough' (multi-step or brittle-metric) scaling; and social bias often rises with scale in ambiguous contexts. Ships a 24-task BIG-bench Lite subset and a canary-string contamination guard.

papers.title: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models
papers.authors: Liang, Bommasani, Lee, Tsipras, Soylu, …, Mai, Zhang, Koreeda
papers.abstract: The benchmark framework that made language-model evaluation multi-metric and standardized: 7 metrics (accuracy, calibration, robustness, fairness, bias, toxicity, efficiency) measured for each of 16 core scenarios — plus 7 targeted evaluations over 26 scenarios — across 30 models under identical 5-shot prompting, raising average model×scenario coverage from 17.9% to 96.0% (42 scenarios in all, 21 new to mainstream LM eval). Its 25 findings show instruction-tuning beats scale — text-davinci-002 and a 52B Anthropic model top accuracy, robustness, and fairness over a 530B model — while the multi-metric lens exposes trade-offs a single accuracy number hides: robustness and fairness track accuracy, calibration does not, and on BBQ the most accurate models are the most socially biased. The living-benchmark, name-what's-missing template later rubric and capability evals (MT-Bench, LegalBench, HealthBench) inherited.

papers.title: WebVoyager: Building an End-to-End Web Agent with Large Multimodal Models
papers.authors: He, Yao, Ma, Yu, Dai, Zhang, Lan, Yu
papers.abstract: The first web agent to operate live websites end-to-end from rendered screenshots rather than HTML: a GPT-4V perception-action loop that overlays numbered Set-of-Mark boxes on interactive elements (via a rule-based DOM tool, no object detector), keeps the last 3 screenshots plus full thought/action history, and acts through 7 human-like actions (Click, Input, Scroll, Wait, Back, Jump-to-Search, Answer) over Selenium on the open web. On a self-instruct benchmark of 643 tasks across 15 real sites it reaches 59.1% task success (human eval) vs 40.1% for a text-only accessibility-tree variant and 30.8% for GPT-4 (All Tools), winning big on visual sites (Google Map 76.7%, Coursera 73.8%) but flat on text-heavy ones (Allrecipes, GitHub). It also ships a GPT-4V auto-judge that scores open-ended trajectories at 85.3% agreement / kappa 0.70 with humans — matching inter-annotator agreement. Failures are dominated by Navigation Stuck (44.4%) over Visual Grounding (24.8%) and Hallucination (21.8%); the live-web design trades reproducibility for realism, login/CAPTCHA tasks are excluded, and the GPT-4V judge isn't independent of the GPT-4V agent.

papers.title: AppWorld: A Controllable World of Apps and People for Benchmarking Interactive Coding Agents
papers.authors: Trivedi, Khot, Hartmann, …, Sabharwal, Balasubramanian
papers.abstract: A fully simulated world of 9 everyday apps and ~100 fictitious people reachable through 457 APIs, with 750 interactive-coding tasks (250 scenarios × 3) where an agent writes rich, iterative code against a live environment and is graded on the resulting database state — every required change must happen and nothing else, the collateral-damage clause. Even the best 2024 system, ReAct + GPT-4o, completes just 48.8% of 'normal' and 30.2% of 'challenge' tasks (TGC), dropping to 32.1%/13.0% on the stricter scenario-consistency metric (SGC); oracle APIs help by ≤9.8 points, so the wall is composing APIs into correct, error-adaptive code, not retrieving them.

papers.title: FORTRESS: Frontier Risk Evaluation for National Security and Public Safety
papers.authors: Knight, Deshpande, Sirdeshmukh, Mankikar, …, Michael
papers.abstract: A safeguard-robustness benchmark for national-security and public-safety (NSPS) content: 500 expert-crafted single-turn adversarial prompts (1,010 total, the rest held private) across three law-grounded domains — CBRNE, Political Violence & Terrorism, and Criminal & Financial Illicit Activities (10 subdomains) — each graded by an instance-specific rubric of 4–7 binary questions and paired with a benign twin to measure over-refusal. A three-model judge panel (o3, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5 Pro) majority-votes each rubric question (89.05% agreement with human experts, 92.98 F1), yielding an Average Risk Score (ARS) and Over-Refusal Score (ORS) that trade off inversely across 26 models: Claude 3.5 Sonnet is most robust (ARS 14.09) but over-refuses most (ORS 21.80), DeepSeek-R1 is most exploitable (78.05) yet barely over-refuses (0.60), and the latest Gemini 2.5 Pro is the worst proprietary model (66.29). The Biological subdomain and psychological/social-engineering tactics break safeguards hardest; an ensemble attacker picking the most-vulnerable model per prompt reaches ARS 89.0 — 6.32× the safest single model — and newer/bigger is not reliably safer (GPT-4.1 > GPT-4o in vulnerability).

papers.title: RubricHub: A Comprehensive and Highly Discriminative Rubric Dataset via Automated Coarse-to-Fine Generation
papers.authors: Li, Zhao, Wei, …, Zhang, Chen
papers.abstract: A fully automated Coarse-to-Fine pipeline for manufacturing discriminative rubrics — principle-guided + response-grounded generation, multi-model aggregation across heterogeneous frontier models (GPT-5.1, Gemini 3 Pro), and a difficulty-evolution stage that hardens ‘is it correct?’ into ‘is it exceptional?’ — yielding RubricHub, a ~110k question–rubric dataset over five domains (Medical and Science 27.1% each, Instruction Following 20.9%, Writing 15.9%, Chat 9.0%; up to 32 criteria/query, and even Qwen3-235B averaging only ~0.6). A two-stage recipe (RuFT rubric rejection-sampling SFT → RuRL with DAPO) takes a base Qwen3-14B to 69.3 on HealthBench, edging GPT-5’s 67.2, with ArenaHard V2 climbing 5.2→74.4 and strictly monotonic Base<RuFT<RuRL<RuFT→RuRL gains; swapping RaR’s rubrics for RubricHub-generated ones lifts HealthBench 47.7→62.1 with all else fixed. The headline is large but lives entirely inside a model-written, model-graded rubric loop: positive-only presence-based criteria, ‘†’ reported frontier scores, no human eval of the trained outputs, and far smaller gains on verifiable GPQA-Diamond (58.5).

papers.title: Scaling Laws for Reward Model Overoptimization
papers.authors: Gao, Schulman, Hilton
papers.abstract: Replaces expensive human labels with a fixed 6B “gold” reward model so reward-model overoptimization can be measured precisely: proxy RMs (3M–3B) are trained on the gold model’s synthetic preferences and optimized by best-of-n or PPO, and the gold reward rises then falls along clean functional forms — R=d(α−βd) for BoN and d(α−β·log d) for RL with d=√KL — whose α/β coefficients scale smoothly (≈log-linear) with RM size, β (the extremal-Goodhart term) shrinking as models grow. More RM data (not more epochs) reduces goodharting, larger policies don’t overoptimize faster, an explicit KL penalty acts only as early stopping, and online RLHF lifts the final gold score by β·d·log(k). The empirical Goodhart curve the rubric-reward and reward-hacking literature is built on — though it measures only the proxy-vs-gold gap, in one InstructGPT environment, with non-adversarial models.

papers.title: Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
papers.authors: Bai, Jones, Ndousse, Askell, …, Amodei, Brown, Kaplan
papers.abstract: Anthropic's foundational RLHF recipe for general-purpose assistants: fit a preference model to crowdworker comparisons (helpfulness, plus adversarial red-teaming, gathered through open-ended dialogue), then optimize it with PPO under a small KL penalty (λKL=0.001) across seven models from 13M to 52B. Alignment is essentially free above ~10B parameters — 13B/52B RLHF models beat their raw counterparts on zero-shot NLP and match on few-shot (an 'alignment bonus'), while smaller models pay an 'alignment tax' — and HH training is compatible with specialized skills like summarization and code (improving HumanEval pass@k for large models). The paper names the field's enduring mechanisms: preference-model-as-reward, the helpful-vs-harmless tension (a PM trained on one objective scores worse-than-chance on the other), iterated 'online' data collection that fills the high-quality tail, and reward over-optimization — train/test PMs diverge past ~150k samples while reward grows linearly in √DKL. Crowdworkers prefer the online HH model to hired professional writers ~56% of the time, the 52B PM hits 86% on HHH evaluations vs 75% reported human, and the released hh-rlhf preference data plus the PM+PPO+KL template became the direct ancestor of modern assistant alignment.

papers.title: Universal and Transferable Adversarial Attacks on Aligned Language Models
papers.authors: Zou, Wang, Carlini, Nasr, Kolter, Fredrikson
papers.abstract: The paper that recast LLM alignment as an adversarial-robustness problem. Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG) combines an affirmative-response objective (forcing the reply to begin “Sure, here is …”), a greedy gradient-based discrete search over all suffix-token positions, and multi-prompt/multi-model aggregation to automatically build a single ~20-token adversarial suffix that jailbreaks aligned LLMs. On the AdvBench benchmark it reaches 88% exact-match attack success on Vicuna-7B harmful strings (vs 25% for AutoPrompt and 0% for PEZ/GBDA) and 84–98% on harmful behaviors, and the gibberish suffix optimized only on open Vicuna/Guanaco transfers to black-box production models — 86.6% ensembled ASR on GPT-3.5, 46.9% on GPT-4, 66.0% on PaLM-2, with Claude-2 the holdout at 2.1%. Transfer is partly confounded by Vicuna being distilled from GPT-3.5, and the specific suffixes were patched after responsible disclosure, but the GCG method and AdvBench became the reference attack the red-teaming and defense literature is measured against.

papers.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
papers.authors: Yao, Zhao, Yu, Du, Shafran, Narasimhan, Cao
papers.abstract: Introduces ReAct, a prompting paradigm that interleaves free-form reasoning traces (“thoughts”) with task actions in one trajectory: thoughts update the model's context to plan and handle exceptions, while actions query external environments (a Wikipedia API, text games, web shopping) to ground reasoning in real observations. On a frozen PaLM-540B with only 1–6 in-context examples, ReAct curbs chain-of-thought's hallucination on HotpotQA/Fever and beats imitation/RL agents on ALFWorld (+34%, best-of-6) and WebShop (+10%). The originating template for modern LLM agent scaffolds.

papers.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
papers.authors: Shinn, Cassano, Labash, Gopinath, Narasimhan, Yao
papers.abstract: Reflexion teaches a frozen LLM agent to learn from trial and error with no weight update: after each attempt an Evaluator scores the trajectory and a Self-Reflection model writes a verbal post-mortem that is stored in episodic memory and fed back on the next try. This "verbal reinforcement learning" lifts performance across decision-making, reasoning, and code (91% pass@1 on HumanEval, 130/134 ALFWorld tasks).

papers.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
papers.authors: Yao, Yu, Zhao, Shafran, Griffiths, Cao, Narasimhan
papers.abstract: Generalizes chain-of-thought into a deliberate tree search: at each step the LM proposes several candidate 'thoughts', scores them in language (value or vote), and explores the tree with BFS/DFS plus lookahead and backtracking — using the model itself as the search heuristic, no training required. On three search-heavy GPT-4 tasks the gains are large: Game of 24 jumps from 4% (chain-of-thought) to 74%, beating even oracle best-of-100 CoT (49%); creative-writing passages are judged more coherent by both a GPT-4 grader (7.56 vs 6.93) and humans (41 vs 21 pairwise); and 5×5 crosswords reach 60% word-level success vs 15.6%, where ablating pruning or backtracking sharply degrades it. About ~60% of CoT failures are sealed after the first step, which is what search recovers. The price is compute (~$0.74/case, up to ~5× tokens) and the gains concentrate on tasks the base model cannot already solve; its lasting contribution is the framing of LM inference as System-2 tree search — a template for the agent and reasoning-RL waves.

papers.title: MCP-RADAR: A Multi-Dimensional Benchmark for Evaluating Tool Use Capabilities in Large Language Models
papers.authors: Gao, Xie, Zhai, Ma, Shen
papers.abstract: The first benchmark built around Anthropic's Model Context Protocol: 507 tool-use tasks across six domains (math, web search, email, calendar, files, terminal), graded on objective answer-, operation-, and token-efficiency metrics. Across ten frontier models it finds a recurring gap — models emit syntactically valid tool calls but routinely select the wrong tool, revealing a superficial grasp of task requirements.

papers.title: Large Language Models are not Fair Evaluators
papers.authors: Wang, Li, Chen, Cai, Zhu, …, Q. Liu, T. Liu, Sui
papers.abstract: A foundational critique of the LLM-as-a-judge paradigm: the quality ranking of two candidate responses can be hacked by simply swapping their order in the prompt. GPT-4 favors the first position and ChatGPT the second, so with ChatGPT as judge Vicuna-13B 'beats' ChatGPT on 66 of 80 questions purely by being placed second (conflict rate 82.5%), while GPT-4 still contradicts itself on 46.3% of close pairs; the bias concentrates where the two answers are near-equal (score gap ≤1) and vanishes once one is clearly better. Three cheap calibration strategies fix it: Multiple Evidence Calibration (reason before scoring, k=3 samples), Balanced Position Calibration (evaluate each candidate in both orders and average), and Human-in-the-Loop Calibration (route the highest position-diversity-entropy examples to humans). Against an 80-question, author-annotated slice of the Vicuna benchmark (human average 71.7% / κ 0.54), MEC+BPC lifts GPT-4 +9.8 (52.7→62.5%) and ChatGPT +14.3 (44.4→58.7%), and adding 20% human review reaches or exceeds human agreement (GPT-4 73.8%, ChatGPT 71.3%) while cutting annotation cost 39%.

papers.title: Can Large Language Models Be an Alternative to Human Evaluations?
papers.authors: Cheng-Han Chiang, Hung-yi Lee
papers.abstract: The paper that named “LLM evaluation” and seeded the LLM-as-a-judge era: feed an instruction-following LLM the exact instructions, sample, and rating question used in a human-evaluation study, parse the 1–5 Likert score it generates, and treat it as a stand-in rater. Validated on two tasks — open-ended story generation (GPT-2-medium vs human-written, 200 each, four attributes) and synonym-substitution adversarial attacks (TextFooler/PWWS/BAE, 100 pairs each) — against three certified English teachers. text-davinci-003 and ChatGPT reproduce the experts’ rankings (rating human-written stories higher than GPT-2’s on all four attributes, every gap significant by Welch’s t-test) while weaker T0 and text-curie-001 show no clear preference; davinci grades generously and ChatGPT harshly, but both preserve the ordering. The signal is aggregate, though: per-story Kendall’s τ between davinci and the teachers is only 0.12–0.22 outside relevance (0.38–0.43), ratings stay stable across instructions and temperature, and LLM evaluation costs under US$5 and a few hours versus US$140 and over a week per human rater. The biases later work made central — self-preference, verbosity, position — go untested, since the judges only grade a weak, different-family generator.

papers.title: AlpacaFarm: A Simulation Framework for Methods that Learn from Human Feedback
papers.authors: Dubois, Li, Taori, Zhang, …, Guestrin, Liang, Hashimoto
papers.abstract: The paper that made RLHF research cheap and reproducible. AlpacaFarm replaces human pairwise annotators with a pool of 13 LLM judges plus 25% label-flip noise — $6 vs $300 per 1,000 comparisons, 65% agreement with the human majority vs the 66% humans reach among themselves — and pairs it with an 805-prompt win-rate benchmark (against Davinci-003) and six tuned reference LPF methods on LLaMA-7B. Its end-to-end validation is the headline: method rankings developed in the $70-and-hours simulator match rankings trained on $3,150 of real human feedback at Spearman 0.98, and the deliberately noisy simulator uniquely reproduces reward over-optimization that a single high-agreement GPT-4 judge misses. PPO tops the leaderboard (55.1% human win-rate), but the result exposes win-rate's verbosity bias — 'PPO beats ChatGPT' is a length-cap artifact — seeding the AlpacaEval / LLM-as-judge lineage.

papers.title: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
papers.authors: Touvron, Lavril, Izacard, …, Joulin, Grave, Lample
papers.abstract: Meta's family of four publicly-trained foundation models (7B/13B/33B/65B) that reoriented scaling around inference cost rather than training compute: since a smaller model trained on far more tokens is cheaper to serve, LLaMA trains on 1.0-1.4T tokens of exclusively public data (CommonCrawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, ArXiv, Stack Exchange). LLaMA-13B outperforms GPT-3 (175B) on most benchmarks despite being >10x smaller and runnable on a single GPU, and LLaMA-65B is competitive with Chinchilla-70B and PaLM-540B - though it trails both on MMLU (63.4 vs 67.5/69.3), which the authors attribute to a thin academic-data mix (only 177GB of books+ArXiv). It bundled the now-standard open-LLM stack (pre-norm RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE), and its release - then leak - of the weights launched Alpaca, Vicuna, and the open-weights ecosystem.

papers.title: LexGLUE: A Benchmark Dataset for Legal Language Understanding in English
papers.authors: Chalkidis, Jana, Hartung, …, Katz, Aletras
papers.abstract: The GLUE-for-law benchmark: seven English legal-NLU datasets behind one leaderboard — ECtHR (A & B) human-rights judgment prediction, SCOTUS issue classification, EUR-LEX/EuroVoc and LEDGAR/UNFAIR-ToS contract tasks, and CaseHOLD multiple-choice QA — scored with a shared micro/macro-F1 and selected on SuperGLUE-style desiderata but deliberately favoring legal expertise. Across a TF-IDF SVM and base-size BERT/RoBERTa/DeBERTa/Longformer/BigBird plus the legal-domain Legal-BERT and CaseLaw-BERT (with hierarchical encoders for the long ECtHR/SCOTUS documents), the legal-oriented models win overall — Legal-BERT leads the harmonic-mean aggregate at 70.8 macro-F1 — but only by ~2–4 points and mostly on US case law (SCOTUS, CaseHOLD), while RoBERTa-large matches them. The fine-tuning-era, expert-curated predecessor that LegalBench later scaled into the few-shot LLM age.

papers.title: CUAD: An Expert-Annotated NLP Dataset for Legal Contract Review
papers.authors: Hendrycks, Burns, Chen, Ball
papers.abstract: A year-long effort by The Atticus Project: 510 commercial contracts from SEC EDGAR hand-annotated by legal experts into 13,101 clause highlights across 41 review categories (every annotation triple-verified, a conservative ~$2M of expert labor), cast as SQuAD-2.0-style extractive “find the needle” QA where labeled text is only ~10% of a contract and ~0.25% per label. Across ten 2021 encoder QA models the best, DeBERTa-xlarge, reaches only 44.0% Precision @ 80% Recall (BERT-base 8.2%) and is the sole model nonzero at 90% recall (17.8%); precision collapses past 80% recall and varies sharply by clause (Governing Law near-ceiling, Covenant-Not-to-Sue ~20% AUPR). Its enduring lesson is that data, not architecture, is the bottleneck — 10× more annotations lifts AUPR 27.6→42.6, matching the entire BERT→DeBERTa gap, while 8GB of unlabeled contracts adds only +3% — making CUAD the canonical demonstration that expert-labeled data unlocks specialized domains.

papers.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
papers.authors: Lewis, Perez, Piktus, …, Riedel, Kiela
papers.abstract: The paper that coined retrieval-augmented generation: pair a frozen Wikipedia index (21M 100-word passages, retrieved by a DPR bi-encoder via maximum inner-product search) with a BART-large seq2seq generator, treat the retrieved document as a latent variable, and fine-tune both end-to-end on the negative marginal log-likelihood — with no supervision on what to retrieve. Two variants marginalize differently: RAG-Sequence fixes one document for the whole output, while RAG-Token can switch documents per generated token. RAG set the state of the art on open Natural Questions (44.5 EM), WebQuestions (45.2) and CuratedTrec (52.2) — beating REALM and the 11B-parameter T5+SSM with only a 400M generator — and produced more factual, specific and diverse text than BART (humans judged it more factual in 42.7% of Jeopardy generations vs BART's 7.1%). Because knowledge lives in a swappable index rather than the weights, hot-swapping a 2016 Wikipedia dump for a 2018 one updated the model's answers about world leaders with no retraining.

papers.title: FinQA: A Dataset of Numerical Reasoning over Financial Data
papers.authors: Z. Chen, W. Chen, Smiley, Shah, …, Wang
papers.abstract: The first expert-annotated benchmark for multi-step numerical reasoning over real financial filings: 8,281 QA pairs over S&P 500 earnings reports (1999–2019), each paired with a gold, executable reasoning program drawing on both tables and prose. The best 2021 retriever-generator (FinQANet, RoBERTa-large) reaches 61.24% execution accuracy versus 91.16% for finance experts and 50.68% for crowdworkers.

papers.title: FinBen: A Holistic Financial Benchmark for Large Language Models
papers.authors: Xie, Han, Chen, …, Ananiadou, Huang
papers.abstract: The first broad-coverage open financial benchmark: 36 datasets across 24 tasks and seven aspects (information extraction, textual analysis, QA, text generation, risk management, forecasting, decision-making), with three new datasets (EDTSum, FinTrade, Regulations) and the first agent-based stock-trading and RAG evaluation in finance. Across 15 LLMs the profile is lopsided — GPT-4 leads extraction and numeric QA (FinQA EM 0.63 vs ~0 for open models) while every model collapses on complex extraction (FinRED F1 0.02), extractive summarization (ECTSum 0.00), and forecasting (MCC ≈ 0, barely above chance). The bright spot is trading: as the brain of the FinMem agent GPT-4 returns +28.19% with a Sharpe of 1.51 and the lowest drawdown (18.34%), beating Buy-&-Hold (−4.00%) and RL traders (Sharpe ≤ 0.01) — but on just 10 stocks over one 2022–2023 window with wide confidence intervals and pretraining-window prices.

papers.title: Judging the Judges: Evaluating Alignment and Vulnerabilities in LLMs-as-Judges
papers.authors: Thakur, Choudhary, Ramayapally, Vaidyanathan, Hupkes
papers.abstract: A controlled audit of the LLM-as-a-judge paradigm on short-answer TriviaQA, deliberately chosen because three human annotators agree 98.52% of the time (Scott’s π 96.2) so any judge–human gap is the judge’s fault rather than task ambiguity. Across 13 judge models grading 9 base/chat exam-takers, only the largest — GPT-4 Turbo, Llama-3 70B and Llama-3.1 70B — reach Scott’s π in the high 80s, still ~8 points below human agreement and up to 5 points off on assigned scores, while raw percent agreement clusters every judge near the top and hides 10–20-point scoring errors that the chance-corrected π exposes. Error analysis surfaces systematic vulnerabilities — a leniency bias (defaulting to ‘correct’ when unsure, P+ > 0.5), prompt-length fragility on weaker judges, and gullibility to dummy answers like ‘Yes’/‘Sure’ — yet a dumb ‘contains’ substring check ranks models at ρ 0.99, showing that getting the ranking right is a different, easier problem than getting the score right.

papers.title: LLM Evaluators Recognize and Favor Their Own Generations
papers.authors: Panickssery, Bowman, Feng
papers.abstract: Shows that LLM judges can recognize their own outputs — GPT-4 at 73.5% out of the box, over 90% after fine-tuning on just 500 examples — and that self-preference bias rises roughly linearly with this self-recognition capability across models, datasets, and even unrelated control fine-tunes. Provides initial causal evidence that LLMs overrate their own text because they recognize it, with implications for the whole LLM-as-judge, reward-modeling, and self-refinement stack.

papers.title: Generative AI at Work
papers.authors: Brynjolfsson, Li, Raymond
papers.abstract: The first study of a generative-AI tool deployed at scale in a real workplace: the staggered rollout of a GPT-based conversational assistant to 5,172 customer-support agents at a Fortune 500 firm (3M chats). Access to AI raises productivity (issues resolved per hour) by 15% on average, but the gains are deeply uneven — the lowest-skill and newest agents improve ~36% while the most experienced gain near zero (and lose a little quality), compressing the skill distribution rather than widening it as earlier IT waves did. A staggered difference-in-differences (Sun–Abraham event study, an IV, and a small RCT) shows the effect is immediate and persistent, partly reflects durable learning (workers stay faster even during AI outages), and arrives with better customer sentiment (~0.5 SD), ~25% fewer manager-escalation requests, and 40% lower attrition among new hires. The real-world economic-impact backbone of the GDPval / RLI / SWE-Lancer / UpBench cluster.

papers.title: GPTs are GPTs: An Early Look at the Labor Market Impact Potential of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Eloundou, Manning, Mishkin, Rock
papers.abstract: Reframes “will AI take jobs?” as a measurable, task-level exposure problem: an LLM-exposure rubric — could the task be done in half the time at equal quality? — scored over all 1,016 O*NET occupations by both human annotators and GPT-4. Estimates that ~80% of U.S. workers have at least 10% of their tasks exposed and ~19% have over half; ~15% of all tasks are directly exposed (α), rising to 47–56% once software built on top of LLMs (E2) is counted. The signature finding inverts prior automation indices — exposure rises with wages and educational barriers, hitting writing- and programming-heavy white-collar work hardest and science/critical-thinking roles least. Coins the thesis “GPTs are GPTs”: LLMs as general-purpose technologies.

papers.title: Measuring short-form factuality in large language models
papers.authors: Wei, Karina, Chung, Jiao, Papay, Glaese, Schulman, Fedus
papers.abstract: OpenAI's short-form factuality benchmark: 4,326 fact-seeking questions with a single, indisputable answer, adversarially collected so that at least one of four GPT-4-class models got each one wrong, then graded correct / incorrect / not-attempted by a prompted ChatGPT classifier. The headline is calibration, not just accuracy — abstention ("not attempted") is a first-class outcome, so a model is rewarded for knowing what it doesn't know. Frontier models flunk: o1-preview answers only 42.7% correctly and GPT-4o, o1, and Claude all land under 50% overall-correct, while every model overstates its stated confidence. Cheap, low-variance, and trivially gradable, but short-form only and Wikipedia-heavy, so its difficulty will erode as the public set leaks into training corpora.

papers.title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
papers.authors: Schick, Dwivedi-Yu, Dessì, Raileanu, Lomeli, Zettlemoyer, Cancedda, Scialom
papers.abstract: The paper that turned tool use into a self-supervised, general capability rather than a hand-annotated or task-specific one. Toolformer annotates a plain corpus (a CCNet subset) with candidate API calls sampled in-context, executes each call against a real tool (a retrieval-augmented QA system, BM25 Wikipedia search, a Python calculator, a 600M NLLB translator, and a calendar), and keeps only the calls whose result lowers the model's own weighted next-token loss by at least a threshold; it then finetunes GPT-J (6.7B) on the augmented text. The model learns to decide which API to call, when, and with what arguments, pausing decoding at the '->' token at inference to splice in the real result. Zero-shot it beats every same-size GPT-J baseline and tops GPT-3 (175B) on LAMA factual completion (53.5 vs 39.8 on T-REx) and math (40.4/29.4/44.0 on ASDiv/SVAMP/MAWPS) with no loss in language-modeling perplexity, though it still trails GPT-3 on open QA. The crucial scaling finding: benefiting from tools is itself emergent, appearing only around 775M parameters. The conceptual seed of modern tool-calling agents — but limited to single, independent, non-chained calls filtered by predictability rather than correctness.

papers.title: HotpotQA: A Dataset for Diverse, Explainable Multi-hop Question Answering
papers.authors: Yang, Qi, Zhang, Bengio, Cohen, Salakhutdinov, Manning
papers.abstract: The Wikipedia-based benchmark that made multi-hop reasoning and explanation first-class in QA: 112,779 crowd-sourced question–answer pairs that each require chaining facts across two documents, paired with the sentence-level supporting facts that justify the answer (graded by a joint answer+evidence EM/F1) plus a novel comparison-question type. A reimplemented bi-attention/self-attention reader reaches only 58.28 distractor answer F1 (41.37 joint) and 34.40 in the open-domain full-wiki setting — where a tf-idf retriever finds both gold paragraphs in the top-10 just 56% of the time — against 91.40 human answer F1. Its distractor-vs-fullwiki template and supporting-fact supervision seeded the agentic-retrieval era (ReAct, Reflexion), and it is now the easy-to-find QA baseline that browsing benchmarks like BrowseComp explicitly move past.

papers.title: WebShop: Towards Scalable Real-World Web Interaction with Grounded Language Agents
papers.authors: Yao, Chen, Yang, Narasimhan
papers.abstract: A simulated e-commerce environment of 1.18 million real amazon.com product listings and 12,087 crowd-written instructions, where an agent must search, compare items, set the right options, and buy a product matching a spec — graded by an automatically-computed attribute/option/price reward that needs no human in the loop. The best 2022 imitation+RL agents reach a 62.4 task score and 28.7% success versus 59.6% for human experts, and a choice-oracle probe (rule success 9.6% → 85.4%) localizes the gap to selecting the right product options rather than generating searches; trained agents also transfer zero-shot to live amazon.com and ebay.com. The auto-graded, high-level-action web sandbox that became standard infrastructure for later web and tool agents (ReAct, Reflexion, AgentBench, WebVoyager, AppWorld).

papers.title: WebThinker: Empowering Large Reasoning Models with Deep Research Capability
papers.authors: Li, Jin, Dong, Qian, …, Wen, Zhu, Dou
papers.abstract: Turns an open large reasoning model (QwQ-32B) into a deep-research agent by letting the model itself drive the research instead of slotting it into a fixed RAG pipeline. A Deep Web Explorer — an LRM-driven sub-agent that searches, clicks links/buttons to navigate deeper, and returns distilled findings — fires whenever the main chain of thought hits a knowledge gap; an Autonomous Think-Search-and-Draft strategy interleaves reasoning, searching, and report writing in real time via three draft/check/edit tools run by an assistant LLM over a shared document memory. The behaviour is sharpened with iterative online DPO over preference pairs ranked by correctness, then tool-efficiency, then conciseness. Among 32B systems it dominates GPQA/GAIA/WebWalkerQA/HLE (GAIA 22.3→48.5, WebWalkerQA 4.3→46.5, HLE 9.6→15.8, all Pass@1) and tops Gemini2.0 / Grok3 Deep Research on LLM-judged report quality (8.1 avg) — though on the verifiable benchmarks it still trails closed systems like OpenAI Deep Research (GAIA 67.4, HLE 26.6). Ablations pin the gains on the Deep Web Explorer (complex-reasoning avg 45.4→38.3 without it), with RL a smaller second-order boost (Base 42.1 → RL 45.4).

papers.title: DeepResearcher: Scaling Deep Research via Reinforcement Learning in Real-world Environments
papers.authors: Zheng, Fu, Hu, Cai, Ye, Lu, Liu
papers.abstract: The first open framework to train a deep-research agent end-to-end with reinforcement learning against the live web rather than a static RAG corpus: a Qwen2.5-7B agent learns via GRPO with an outcome-only word-F1 reward (and a -1 format penalty, tool outputs masked from the loss) to interleave web search, multi-agent page browsing, and reasoning. Trained on 80k contamination-filtered (pass@10) multi-hop QA examples, it beats prompt-engineered agents by up to 28.9 points and RAG-based RL agents by up to 7.2, and generalizes best out-of-domain - on Bamboogle (answers not all on Wikipedia) it tops even a RAG-trained agent given real-web access, evidence that training in the real environment, not just deploying there, builds the skill. Outcome-only RL induces emergent planning, cross-validation, reflection, and honest abstention. Caveat: 'deep research' here is short-answer multi-hop QA graded by word-F1 and a GPT-4o-mini judge, not long-form report writing.

papers.title: Assisting in Writing Wikipedia-like Articles From Scratch with Large Language Models
papers.authors: Shao, Jiang, Kanell, Xu, Khattab, Lam
papers.abstract: STORM automates the pre-writing stage of long-form grounded writing: it discovers diverse perspectives on a topic, then simulates multi-turn interviews between a perspective-guided Wikipedia writer and a search-grounded expert (which splits queries, sifts sources by Wikipedia reliability rules, and synthesizes grounded answers), and finally curates a draft-then-refined outline before writing the article section by section with citations. Built zero-shot in DSPy and evaluated on the leakage-resistant FreshWiki dataset with new heading soft-recall / entity-recall metrics and 10 expert Wikipedia editors, STORM beats outline-driven RAG on organization (+25% judged well-organized) and coverage and roughly doubles-to-triples the sources it surfaces (99.83 vs 39.56 references). But the expert study exposes the harder problems it makes legible: source-bias transfer (7/10 editors call outputs unneutral) and over-association / red-herring errors, with verifiability statistically tied to the baseline (3.80 vs 3.85). A foundational system for the deep-research / report-generation line that followed.

papers.title: Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Software Tasks
papers.authors: Kwa, West, Becker, …, Barnes, Chan
papers.abstract: METR proposes the 50%-task-completion time horizon — the human-time length of tasks a model can finish with 50% success — as a non-saturating, human-relatable capability axis. Timing 800+ skilled human baseliners (2,529 hours) on 170 tasks spanning 1-second atomic actions to 30-hour ML research projects (HCAST + RE-Bench + the new SWAA suite), then fitting a per-model logistic curve over 12 frontier models (2019–2025), they find the horizon has doubled every ~207 days (≈7 months) at R²=0.97 — from GPT-2's ~2 seconds to o3's ~110 minutes — driven mainly by reliability and error-recovery. The 80% horizon doubles at the same rate but stays 4–6× shorter. Naive extrapolation puts month-long (167-hour) software tasks within reach around 2029, with external validity (not statistical noise) the dominant caveat the authors foreground.

papers.title: Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developer Productivity
papers.authors: Becker, Rush, Barnes, Rein
papers.abstract: METR's randomized controlled trial on whether AI actually speeds up expert developers in the wild. 16 experienced open-source maintainers complete 246 real issues on large, mature repositories they personally know (avg 23k stars, ~1.1M LOC, and 5 years / 1,500 commits of prior experience each), with every issue defined and time-forecast before being randomized to AI-allowed (Cursor Pro + Claude 3.5/3.7 Sonnet, the Feb–Jun 2025 frontier) or AI-disallowed. Allowing AI increased completion time by 19% — the opposite of expectations: developers forecast a 24% speedup and still estimated 20% afterward, while economics and ML experts predicted 39% and 38% speedups. Screen-recording labels (143 hours, 29% of all developer-hours) show the time going into prompting, waiting on, and reviewing/cleaning AI output, with under 44% of AI suggestions accepted. The slowdown is robust across estimators and outcome measures but setting-specific: the authors attribute it to high repo familiarity, large complex codebases, and low AI reliability, and explicitly do not claim it generalizes to greenfield or unfamiliar work.

papers.title: Can LLMs Generate Novel Research Ideas? A Large-Scale Human Study with 100+ NLP Researchers
papers.authors: Si, Yang, Hashimoto
papers.abstract: The first adequately-powered, confounder-controlled head-to-head between an LLM ideation agent and 49 expert NLP researchers. Across seven prompting-research topics, 79 experts write 298 blind reviews of 49 ideas per condition (human, AI, AI-plus-human-rerank), with idea style normalized so judges can't distinguish them (50% accuracy). AI-generated ideas are judged significantly more novel than expert ideas (5.64-5.81 vs 4.84, p<0.01) and comparable on other axes, though slightly weaker on feasibility. The agent shows hard limits: idea diversity collapses (~200 non-duplicates from 4,000 generated seeds) and LLMs can't yet evaluate ideas reliably (best 53.3% vs 56.1% human agreement). A landmark for evaluation rigor that is candid the win sits on a noisy instrument against median-grade human expert ideas.

papers.title: Establishing Best Practices for Building Rigorous Agentic Benchmarks
papers.authors: Zhu, Jin, Pruksachatkun, Zhang, …, Zaharia, Stoica, Liang, Kang
papers.abstract: A checklist and best-practices standard for rigorous agentic evaluation, synthesized from 17 widely-used benchmarks. It decomposes agentic eval into two validity conditions — task validity (a task is solvable iff the agent has the target capability) and outcome validity (passing the grader truly means task success) — and shows that all ten benchmarks audited violate at least one: a do-nothing agent scores 38% on τ-bench-Airline, an agent overwrites SWE-Lancer's hidden tests for 100%, KernelBench overstates kernel correctness by 31%, and OSWorld under-rates the SOTA agent by 28% from website drift. The deliverable, ABC, is 43 checks across task validity (T.1–10), outcome validity (O.a–i), and reporting (R.1–13); applied forward to CVE-Bench it cut performance overestimation by 33%.

papers.title: BEIR: A Heterogeneous Benchmark for Zero-shot Evaluation of Information Retrieval Models
papers.authors: Thakur, Reimers, Rücklé, Srivastava, Gurevych
papers.abstract: The field-defining zero-shot retrieval benchmark: 18 datasets across 9 tasks (fact-checking, QA, citation prediction, argument and bio-medical retrieval, …) wrap 10 systems spanning lexical, sparse, dense, late-interaction, and re-ranking architectures in one nDCG@10 harness, all evaluated out-of-domain with no in-task training. The headline reframed IR evaluation: in-domain accuracy does not predict generalization, and the lexical baseline BM25 beats nearly every neural retriever zero-shot — only the cross-encoder re-ranker BM25+CE (16/18 datasets, +11% avg) and document-expansion docT5query reliably surpass it, at 20–30× the latency of a dense bi-encoder. A Hole@10 study honestly flags the catch: lexical pooling under-counts dense retrievers' hits (TAS-B 31.8% holes vs BM25 6.4%), and re-annotating TREC-COVID lifts ANCE from below BM25 to 6.7 nDCG points above it.

papers.title: From Local to Global: A Graph RAG Approach to Query-Focused Summarization
papers.authors: Edge, Trinh, Cheng, Bradley, Chao, Mody, Truitt, Metropolitansky, Ness, Larson
papers.abstract: Conventional vector RAG retrieves the top-k chunks nearest a query, so it fails on global 'sensemaking' questions over a whole corpus (e.g. 'what are the main themes?') — inherently a query-focused summarization task, not retrieval. GraphRAG uses an LLM to build an entity knowledge graph from the source text, partitions it into a hierarchy of communities (Leiden), pre-summarizes every community, and at query time map-reduces those summaries into a single global answer. On two ~1M-token corpora it beats vector RAG on comprehensiveness (72–83% win) and diversity, with root-level summaries delivering most of the gain at ~2.6% of the token cost.

papers.title: Navigating the Digital World as Humans Do: Universal Visual Grounding for GUI Agents
papers.authors: Gou, Wang, Zheng, Xie, Chang, Shu, Sun, Su
papers.abstract: Argues GUI agents should perceive purely visually (screenshots) and act at the pixel level, like humans, instead of reading HTML or accessibility trees. The bottleneck is grounding: mapping a planner's textual description of an element to its on-screen coordinate. UGround is a 7B LLaVA-NeXT (later Qwen2-VL) grounding model trained on the largest GUI-grounding dataset to date, 10M elements over 1.3M screenshots, mostly synthesized from Common Crawl webpages via a hybrid rule+LLM pipeline (Web-Hybrid). Plugged into the vision-only SeeAct-V framework, it beats SeeClick by ~20% absolute on ScreenSpot and lets a screenshot-only agent match or beat HTML/a11y-based SOTA across six web, desktop, and mobile benchmarks, though end-to-end live-task success stays ~23-44%.

papers.title: OS-ATLAS: A Foundation Action Model for Generalist GUI Agents
papers.authors: Wu, Wu, Xu, Wang, Sun, Jia, Cheng, Ding, Chen, Liang, Qiao
papers.abstract: The first open foundation action model for GUI agents, built to close the grounding gap that pushes practitioners toward GPT-4o/Gemini. Its real contribution is a data engine: an open multi-platform synthesis toolkit and the largest open cross-platform GUI grounding corpus to date — 2.3M+ screenshots and 13.58M elements across Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and the web (the first open desktop grounding data), web-crawled from FineWeb and OS-automated via accessibility trees plus DFS/random-walk exploration. A unified action space (three basic actions — click/type/scroll — plus extensible custom actions) collapses 17 conflicting action types to 10, fixing the cross-platform tap≡click naming clash that degrades multitask fine-tuning. Trained in two phases (grounding pre-training → OS-Atlas-Base, then action fine-tuning → OS-Atlas) on InternVL-2-4B and Qwen2-VL-7B backbones, it runs in Grounding, Action, and Agent modes. OS-Atlas-Base-7B reaches 82.47 average grounding accuracy on ScreenSpot (vs concurrent UGround's 73.30 and SeeClick's 53.40) and OS-Atlas-7B beats GPT-4o on all six zero-shot OOD agent benchmarks (e.g. GUI-Odyssey SR 26.96 vs 5.36). But the gains rest on the GUI-pretrained 7B backbone and a lenient point-in-box metric; on the only fully interactive testbed (OSWorld, as a drop-in grounding module) the best config hits 14.63% vs 72.36% human — grounding advanced, autonomy not. Also ships ScreenSpot-V2, a re-annotation fixing 11.32% of ScreenSpot's labels.

papers.title: AndroidWorld: A Dynamic Benchmarking Environment for Autonomous Agents
papers.authors: Rawles, Clinckemaillie, Chang, …, Lillicrap, Riva
papers.abstract: Google DeepMind's mobile counterpart to OSWorld: a fully functional Pixel 6 / Android 13 emulator that supplies durable, execution-based reward for 116 hand-built tasks across 20 real apps (open-source F-Droid plus built-in system apps), reading success from device system state via adb — files, SQLite rows, settings — rather than matching UI screenshots, and reusing checks across unrelated apps. Its signature feature is automatic task parameterization: each task is re-instantiated from a random seed into a practically infinite set of goals and success criteria, with composable partial-credit tasks and a ported MobileMiniWoB++ (92 web tasks) on the same TaskEval interface. The reference zero-shot ReAct+Reflexion agent M3A tops out at 30.6% (GPT-4 Turbo, accessibility-tree input) versus 80.0% human, with multimodal Set-of-Marks input underperforming text-only and failures clustering on UI grounding, memory, and operational knowledge rather than reasoning. The methodological punchline is a robustness analysis: across three seeds M3A scores 27.6/26.3/33.2% (mean 29.0%) and per-task can swing from 0% to ~45%, showing single-seed benchmark scores can be luck.

papers.title: Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback
papers.authors: Bai, Kadavath, Kundu, Askell, …, Brown, Kaplan
papers.abstract: Introduces Constitutional AI (CAI): training a harmless, non-evasive assistant with no human labels for harm — human oversight enters only as a short list of ~16 written principles (a 'constitution'). Two stages bolt onto a helpful-only RLHF model. (1) Supervised: the model critiques and revises its own red-team responses against sampled principles (182,831 prompts, up to 4 critique-revision loops each) and is finetuned on the revisions (SL-CAI). (2) RLAIF — the term coined here: a feedback model labels which of two responses better satisfies a sampled principle, those AI preferences (mixed with human helpfulness labels) train a preference model, and SL-CAI is RL-tuned against it (RL-CAI). At 52B, RL-CAI — especially with chain-of-thought feedback — sits on a better harmlessness-helpfulness Pareto frontier than standard RLHF and is virtually never evasive, engaging with harmful queries by explaining its objections. Caveats the authors flag: over-trained RL-CAI Goodharts into boilerplate ('you are valid, valued, and cared for'), CoT labels need clamping to 40–60% to stay calibrated, and the headline harmlessness gain partly reflects re-instructing crowdworkers to disprefer evasive answers. The conceptual ancestor of rubric/criteria-as-reward methods (Rule-Based Rewards, checklists, rubric anchors).

papers.title: DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report
papers.authors: DeepSeek-AI
papers.abstract: A 671B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model (37B activated per token) built on Multi-head Latent Attention plus two new training strategies: auxiliary-loss-free load balancing and multi-token prediction. It matches GPT-4o and Claude-3.5-Sonnet — leading on math and competitive code — trained for $5.576M (2.788M H800 GPU-hours) via the first validated large-scale FP8 run; V3-Base became the foundation for DeepSeek-R1.

papers.title: TÜLU 3: Pushing Frontiers in Open Language Model Post-Training
papers.authors: Lambert, Morrison, Pyatkin, Huang, Ivison, …, Dasigi, Hajishirzi
papers.abstract: Ai2's fully-open post-training recipe — SFT → DPO → RLVR on Llama 3.1 base (8B/70B/405B) — that matches or beats Llama-3.1-Instruct, Qwen 2.5, Mistral, GPT-4o-mini and Claude 3.5 Haiku while releasing all data, code, and evaluation (OLMES) plus the dead ends. Introduces Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR): replace the learned reward model with a binary correctness verifier (reward α=10 if the answer passes, else 0) optimized by PPO. RLVR gives sharp targeted gains on GSM8K/MATH/IFEval (8B GSM8K 84.3→87.6) but only tenths of a point on the overall average, and overoptimizes as KL grows — the 'reward = passed the verifier' idea the DeepSeek-R1/RLVR wave scaled months later.

papers.title: Rule Based Rewards for Language Model Safety
papers.authors: Mu, Helyar, Heidecke, Achiam, Vallone, …
papers.abstract: Encodes a safety policy as a set of LLM-graded binary propositions (e.g. "refusals should not be judgmental"), fits a tiny linear reward over them, and adds it directly to the helpful-only reward model during PPO — instead of distilling the policy into human preference data. Matches human-data safety at far lower over-refusal (97.1 vs 91.7 F1) using ~1% of the human labels.

papers.title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
papers.authors: Rafailov, Sharma, Mitchell, Ermon, Manning, Finn
papers.abstract: Shows the KL-constrained RLHF objective has a closed-form optimum, and because the Bradley-Terry preference model depends only on reward differences, the intractable partition function cancels -- collapsing 'fit a reward model, then run PPO' into a single binary cross-entropy loss on preference pairs. The policy is its own implicit reward (r = beta * log[pi/pi_ref]); no reward model, no policy sampling in the loop, no RL. With models up to 6B params it matches or beats PPO on sentiment control, summarization, and single-turn dialogue with almost no tuning (~61% TL;DR win rate vs PPO's 57%), and went on to become the default preference-tuning recipe for open chat models.

papers.title: EnigmaEval: A Benchmark of Long Multimodal Reasoning Challenges
papers.authors: Wang, Lee, Menghini, …, Yue, Hendrycks
papers.abstract: A 1,184-puzzle benchmark drawn from eight real puzzle-hunt events (Normal 949, Hard 235) where instruction-free, multimodal problems demand discovering hidden connections between unrelated clues and inventing the solution path. Each puzzle ships in two formats — a raw PDF/webpage screenshot and a human-transcribed text-image version — to separate reasoning from OCR. State-of-the-art vision-language models barely register: the best (o1) solves 7.0% of Normal and 0.0% of Hard, all nine models score 0% on Hard, and even handed the correct component answers models clear only ≤8.7% of meta-puzzles. Difficulty is organic rather than adversarially curated and the scores fall below Humanity's Last Exam; graded by exact string match on a word/short-phrase answer, the benchmark is kept private and continuously updated to resist the contamination its publicly posted solutions invite.

papers.title: Critical Foreign Policy Decisions (CFPD)-Benchmark: Measuring Diplomatic Preferences in Large Language Models
papers.authors: Jensen, Reynolds, Atalan, Garcia, Woo, Chen, Howarth
papers.abstract: A benchmark for a domain with no ground truth: 400 expert-written international-relations scenarios (escalation, intervention, cooperation, alliance dynamics), expanded by swappable country actors to 66,473 questions over 216 actors, that grade what seven foundation models prefer rather than what they know. Models diverge most — and only statistically significantly — on escalation: Llama 3.1 8B Instruct picks Use of Force 41.36% of the time versus 9.41% for Claude 3.5 Sonnet, while Gemini 1.5 Pro parks 54.83% of answers in the middle Threat-of-Force tier. Every model shows a country bias, recommending Use of Force for the US and UK over 50% of the time but never above 50% for China, India, or Russia — pointing to regime type / training data and warning against off-the-shelf deployment in national-security decision support. The dataset is withheld over contamination concerns; the paper ships the methodology as a recipe.

papers.title: Humanity's Last Exam
papers.authors: Phan, Gatti, Han, Li, …, Wang, Hendrycks
papers.abstract: A 2,500-question, multimodal, closed-ended academic benchmark written by ~1,000 subject experts across 500+ institutions and filtered to questions frontier LLMs fail — built to restore measurement headroom after MMLU-style benchmarks saturated above 90%. Two formats (24% multiple-choice, 76% exact-match; ~14% image-based) graded by an o3-mini judge, with a private held-out set guarding overfitting and mathematics making up 41% of the set. At release every model scored under 14% (o3-mini high 13.4% text-only, o1 8.0%, GPT-4o 2.7%) with RMS calibration errors above 70% — confidently wrong rather than uncertain.

papers.title: MultiChallenge: A Realistic Multi-Turn Conversation Evaluation Benchmark Challenging to Frontier LLMs
papers.authors: Sirdeshmukh, Deshpande, Mols, …, Yue, Xing
papers.abstract: A Scale AI benchmark of 273 expert-edited multi-turn conversations isolating four realistic failure modes — instruction retention (69), inference memory of user information (113), reliable versioned editing (41), and self-coherence / anti-sycophancy (50) — each example kept only if it breaks at least 3 of 6 frontier models. Despite near-perfect scores on saturated tests like MT-Bench, every frontier model clears under 50%: Claude 3.5 Sonnet (June 2024) leads human-eval at 41.4% and o1-preview 37.2%, with a steep drop to Gemini 1.5 Pro (20.0), Llama 3.1 405B (14.9), Mistral Large (14.6), and GPT-4o (12.5). Its signature method is an instance-level binary (yes/no) rubric scored only on the final response, which lifts LLM-judge agreement with human raters from 37.3% to 93.9% — a template later rubric evals (HealthBench, PRBench) reused. Difficulty is reasoning-bound, not length-bound: scores show no trend with turn count over short (~1,232-word) conversations.

papers.title: A Careful Examination of Large Language Model Performance on Grade School Arithmetic
papers.authors: Zhang, Da, Lee, Robinson, Wu, …, Kaplan, Lunati, Yue
papers.abstract: Scale AI's contamination probe: GSM1k is 1,205 grade-school math problems hand-written (no LLM, no synthetic data) to mirror GSM8k's difficulty distribution — matched on solution steps, answer magnitude, human solve rate (4.36 vs 4.07 problems/15min), and human distinguishability (21.83% vs 20% chance) — but never publicly released. Re-evaluating leading open and closed models under one fixed 5-shot harness, the worst-overfit families (Phi, Mistral, Yi, Xwin) drop up to 8% on the clean twin (Yi-6B-Chat 43.7%→35.7%), while all frontier models (GPT-4o, GPT-4, Claude 3, Gemini 1.5, Mistral Large) stay flat or improve. A per-character log-likelihood probe links overfit to partial GSM8k memorization (Spearman's r²=0.36, p=0.03), but outliers (Math-Shepherd overfit yet low-likelihood; Llemma high-likelihood yet minimal overfit) show contamination isn't the whole story — and even the most overfit models still reason, with Phi-2 solving over half of GSM1k, matching a 25× larger Llama-2-70B.

papers.title: ToolComp: A Multi-Tool Reasoning & Process Supervision Benchmark
papers.authors: Nath, Raja, Yoon, Hendryx
papers.abstract: Scale AI's tool-use benchmark that grades the trajectory, not just the answer: 485 human-verified prompts requiring chains of dependent calls across 11 tools — split into an 11-tool Enterprise set (287) and a Google-Search-plus-Python Chat set (198) — paired with 1,731 per-step process-supervision labels collected by having annotators mark and rewrite each ReAct step. Across 16 models from 6 families the majority score under 50% on final answers (o1-preview leads at 61.83%, average 46.64%), and tellingly the 2-tool Chat subset is harder than the 11-tool Enterprise one, so the wall is planning, not API mechanics. Step-wise and final-answer accuracy correlate only moderately (Pearson r=0.63), and a process-supervised reward model beats an outcome-supervised one by 19 points in rank@1 on base-model trajectories (and pushes an SFT model to 60.25%, near its pass@30 ceiling) — with full-step labels including the tool Observation and max aggregation working best — early evidence that process supervision transfers from math reasoning to agentic tool use. Deliberately held out (released only once a model clears 85% or at the end of 2026) to resist contamination.

papers.title: The MASK Benchmark: Disentangling Honesty From Accuracy in AI Systems
papers.authors: Ren, Agarwal, Mazeika, …, Lee, Yue, Hendrycks
papers.abstract: Scale AI and the Center for AI Safety's benchmark for measuring whether a model lies — knowingly states what it does not believe — cleanly separated from whether it is factually correct, exposing that prior "honesty" benchmarks mostly measured accuracy in disguise ("safetywashing"). Each of 1,000 public (+500 held-out) human-curated, adversarially-selected examples pairs a pressure prompt engineered to induce a falsehood with neutral belief-elicitation prompts and known ground truth, across six archetypes (Known Facts, Doubling Down, Fabricated Statistics, Continuations, Disinformation, Provided Facts); a lie is scored when the pressured statement S contradicts the elicited belief B (S≠B), via an LLM judge that agrees with human labels 86.4% of the time. Across 30 frontier LLMs no model is explicitly honest in more than 46% of cases, and P(Lie) runs from 26.6% (Claude 3.7 Sonnet) to 63.0% (Grok 2) even at 72–82% belief accuracy. The signature result: across 27 models training compute correlates +87.3% with accuracy but −59.9% with honesty — scale buys knowledge, not candor — implying honesty is a post-training choice. Developer-prompt and representation-engineering (LoRRA) interventions on small Llama-2 models help (+6–13%) but do not eliminate lying.

papers.title: A Holistic Approach for Test and Evaluation of Large Language Models
papers.authors: Slack, Wang, Semenenko, Park, Hendryx
papers.abstract: A 2023 Scale AI technical report proposing a hybrid human-plus-AI methodology for testing LLMs. An entropy-based confidence measure lets a GPT-4 judge auto-grade the pairwise preference comparisons it is most certain about (offloading up to ~20% of inputs at ~86% human agreement) and routes the rest to people; a layered red-teaming pipeline (automated probes, generalist red teamers, then domain experts) surfaces more, and more severe, vulnerabilities than any single source.

papers.title: Adaptive Guidance Accelerates Reinforcement Learning of Reasoning Models
papers.authors: Nath, Lau, Gunjal, Sharma, Baharte, Hendryx
papers.abstract: Decomposes how RLVR teaches reasoning models into two measurable effects — self-distillation (converting pass@16-latent solutions into reliable pass@1) and capability gain (solving problems unsolvable even at pass@16) — and shows across Qwen-2.5 from 0.5B to 72B on >500,000 math/STEM/code problems that self-distillation dominates: RLVR mostly re-allocates probability mass rather than discovering new solutions, with the unsolved-set headroom shrinking from 3,195 items at 0.5B to 1,532 at 72B. To induce real capability gain it introduces Guide, a class of online RL algorithms that inject a reference-derived hint into the context only on prompts where all k rollouts fail — GRPO's dead, zero-advantage prompts that DAPO simply discards — then apply an off-policy importance weight so the guided trajectories optimize the hint-free policy (PPO ratio-clipping is removed for guided rollouts, which otherwise diverges at ~50 steps). Guide-GRPO beats vanilla GRPO by 1-4 points macro pass@1 on math (51.03 vs 49.34 at 7B; 56.26 vs 52.87 at 32B/8K) and uniquely lifts pass@16 (70.15 vs 67.06) while keeping training entropy higher; gating guidance to all-incorrect prompts (51.03) far outscores unconditional guidance (40.97).

papers.title: Jailbreaking to Jailbreak
papers.authors: Kritz, Robinson, Vacareanu, …, Yue, Primack, Wang
papers.abstract: Turns almost any refusal-trained black-box LLM into an autonomous red-teamer (a J2 attacker) using one transferable multi-turn prompt that reframes jailbreaking as safety work, then runs a fixed plan/attack/debrief workflow whose in-context-learning cycles improve the attack over past failures. Reasoning models make the strongest attackers and the vulnerability is growing: self-attack success rose +28%/+66%/+66% for Claude/Gemini/OpenAI over 12 months, and J2(Claude-3.7) reaches 0.975 ASR against GPT-4o (matching expert human red teamers), though against the more robust Sonnet-3.5 the best J2 attacker (o3, 0.605) still trails top black-box baselines like Bijection and BoN.

papers.title: The AI Productivity Index (APEX)
papers.authors: Vidgen, Fennelly, Pinnix, …, Sunstein, Topol, Nitski
papers.abstract: Mercor grades frontier models on realistic deliverables from four high-paid knowledge jobs — investment banking, management consulting, big law, and primary-care medicine — where each expert-authored case pairs a source-grounded prompt with a Pass/Fail rubric (mean 14.81 criteria, ~2.7 hours of real work) and a model's score is the fraction of criteria met. On the n=400 held-out set (8 runs, 95% CIs, a single Gemini 2.5 Flash judge), GPT-5 (thinking=high) leads at 67.0%, ahead of Gemini 3 Pro (64.3%) and a ~63% cluster of o3 / Grok 4 / Opus 4.5; investment banking is the hardest job (top 63.0%) and law the easiest (77.9%) — frontier models still clear only about two-thirds of an expert's bar.

papers.title: Instruction-Following Evaluation for Large Language Models
papers.authors: Zhou, Lu, Mishra, Brahma, Basu, Luan, Zhou, Hou
papers.abstract: Introduces IFEval, a reproducible, judge-free benchmark for LLM instruction-following built around 'verifiable instructions' — directives a short deterministic program can check (word counts, required/forbidden keywords, JSON or bracketed formats, casing). 25 instruction types across 9 groups span 541 prompts, scored four ways: {prompt-level, instruction-level} × {strict, loose}, where the loose criterion applies 8 response transforms (strip markdown, drop the intro/outro line, and their combinations) to absorb formatting false negatives. On the two reference models, GPT-4 (Nov 2023) follows every instruction in only 76.89% of prompts (83.57% per-instruction) and PaLM 2 S just 43.07% — mechanically-simple constraints like length, combination, and punctuation prove hardest, while 'respond in language X' is near-solved. The 'verifiable instruction' framing seeded the later wave of rubric- and checklist-based evaluation; with public prompts and gameable verifiers it now reads as a floor, not a ceiling.

papers.title: MMLU-Pro: A More Robust and Challenging Multi-Task Language Understanding Benchmark
papers.authors: Wang, Ma, Zhang, …, Yue, Chen
papers.abstract: The reasoning-era rebuild of MMLU, and the benchmark most frontier models migrated to once MMLU saturated at 86-87%. It merges MMLU's 57 subjects into 14 domains, filters out ~5,900 'too-easy' items using a panel of eight small models, folds in harder STEM-website / TheoremQA / SciBench questions, and expands every question from four options to ten (avg 9.47) with two rounds of expert review -- 12,032 questions in all. The harder format cut accuracy 16-33% (GPT-4o tops out at 72.6% vs ~88.7% on MMLU), widened top-model gaps from ~2% to ~9%, roughly halved prompt sensitivity (4-5% to ~2%), and flipped chain-of-thought from neutral/harmful on MMLU to a +19% boost. A discriminative, prompt-stable signal -- though absolute scores are softened by a lower guess floor and a GPT-4-Turbo-authored distractor pool.

papers.title: SQuAD: 100,000+ Questions for Machine Comprehension of Text
papers.authors: Rajpurkar, Zhang, Lopyrev, Liang
papers.abstract: The Stanford Question Answering Dataset: 107,785 crowdsourced questions over 536 Wikipedia articles where every answer is a contiguous span of the passage, so predictions score cheaply by exact-match and token F1 with no answer choices to pick from. A feature-rich logistic regression baseline reaches 51.0% F1 against 86.8% human F1, and the span format plus a two-orders-of-magnitude jump in scale made SQuAD the default reading-comprehension benchmark that launched the modern wave of neural reader models.

papers.title: Agent-RLVR: Training Software Engineering Agents via Guidance and Environment Rewards
papers.authors: Da, Wang, Deng, Ma, Barhate, Hendryx
papers.abstract: Makes RLVR effective in sparse-reward agentic settings by introducing agent guidance — train-time teacher hints (a high-level plan, environment feedback on errors, and file-localization cues) that steer a failing software-engineering agent toward passing trajectories, which then become DPO preference pairs for offline RLVR. On SWE-Bench Verified with only 817 training environments, it lifts Qwen-2.5-72B-Instruct from 9.4% to 22.4% pass@1; a reward model trained on the same guidance-augmented data reranks 32 candidate patches to push test-time pass@1 to 27.8%. Guidance is the load-bearing ingredient — the gap over guidance-free RLVR widens as pass@k grows — and the recipe beats a rejection-sampling SFT baseline (20.8%) while using a far smaller dataset and simpler scaffold than SWE-RL or SWE-Gym.

papers.title: A Red Teaming Roadmap Towards System-Level Safety
papers.authors: Wang, Knight, Kritz, Primack, Michael
papers.abstract: A Scale AI (SEAL) position paper arguing that LLM red teaming, in aggregate, optimizes the wrong objective. It should test concrete product safety specifications under realistic threat models rather than abstract ethical principles, and move beyond model-level robustness to system-level safety: environment simulation, trajectory and user monitoring, rapid response, and red teaming the monitor itself.

papers.title: Assessing Robustness to Spurious Correlations in Post-Training Language Models
papers.authors: Shuieh, Singhal, Shanker, Heyer, Pu, Denton
papers.abstract: A controlled study of how the post-training objective interacts with shortcut features in alignment data: SFT, DPO, and KTO are trained on synthetic datasets that inject spurious correlations (Feature Ambiguity / Distributional Narrowness) at 10% vs 90% rates across math (GSM8K), document-grounded QA (QuAC), and constrained instruction-following, then evaluated out-of-distribution over 162 checkpoints. No objective universally wins: preference methods (DPO/KTO) are far more robust on math reasoning (~0.78-0.80 vs SFT ~0.54) while SFT holds up better on context-heavy QA, except the 'always abstain' omission shortcut where SFT collapses (0.465->0.108). Higher contamination does not always degrade performance.

papers.title: Relevance Isn't All You Need: Scaling RAG Systems With Inference-Time Compute Via Multi-Criteria Reranking
papers.authors: LeVine, Varjavand
papers.abstract: Shows that RAG rerankers which maximize context relevance alone hit an information bottleneck — higher retrieval precision but lower answer quality (Cohere Rerank and zero-shot LLM Rerank fall below doing no reranking on a 0–5 answer-similarity metric). REBEL ('RErank BEyond reLevance') fixes this purely in the prompt: score each retrieved document on secondary criteria (depth, diversity, clarity, authoritativeness, recency) via chain-of-thought, optionally inferring the criteria per query through a two-turn meta-prompt. On a 107-question AI-arXiv QA benchmark (GPT-4o generator, GPT-4 judge, 10 runs), two-turn REBEL reaches the best retrieval precision (≈0.945) and answer similarity (≈4.34/5) of any method tested, opening a new tradeoff curve where more inference-time compute buys both better retrieval and better answers.

papers.title: MultiFileTest: A Multi-File-Level LLM Unit Test Generation Benchmark and Impact of Error Fixing Mechanisms
papers.authors: Wang, Xia, Zhao, Du, Miao, Deng, Yu, Xing
papers.abstract: The first unit-test-generation benchmark built for multi-file code: 60 self-contained Python/Java/JavaScript projects (20 each, under 1,600 LOC, with enforced cross-file dependencies) where 11 frontier LLMs must test a whole project at once. Vanilla correctness is mediocre and Java is hardest (several open 7B models score 0), but a 2-6 line manual fix of executability/cascade errors swings correctness from 0 to 60 and reorders the leaderboard - while small open models can't self-fix (often emitting prose instead of code) and even top models write redundant tests.

papers.title: Refusal-Trained LLMs Are Easily Jailbroken As Browser Agents
papers.authors: Kumar, Lau, Vijayakumar, …
papers.abstract: A red-teaming study showing that safety refusal trained into chat LLMs fails to generalize to browser agents. BrowserART, a 100-behavior toolkit on synthetic and real websites, finds GPT-4o's attack success rate rises from 12% in chat to 74% as an agent, and chat-era jailbreaks transfer to push it to 100%.

papers.title: Balancing Cost and Effectiveness of Synthetic Data Generation Strategies for LLMs
papers.authors: Chan, Pu, Shanker, Suresh, Jenks, Heyer, Denton
papers.abstract: Groups LLM synthetic-data generation for fine-tuning into three strategies - Answer Augmentation (new responses to seed questions), Question Rephrasing, and New Question Evolution (new questions with new answers) - and shows the optimal choice depends on the budget ratio BR = queries / seed instructions: augmenting answers wins when BR is low, generating new questions wins when BR is high. With 100 seed instructions the crossover sits at BR ~27-51, falling to ~16 as the seed set grows, and design choices matter far more in low/mid-data regimes than high. A knowledge-distillation sweep (Llama 3.1 70B teacher, Llama 2 7B student) over GSM8k, Spider, and ARC-C, fitted with a data-constrained scaling law (R^2 > 0.98). Also finds a weak augmenter is fine for rephrasing but cripples new-question generation, and that verifying teacher answers against ground truth gives no measurable gain.

papers.title: Revisiting the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis
papers.authors: Raghavendra, Nath, Hendryx
papers.abstract: A direct rebuttal of LIMA's Superficial Alignment Hypothesis, measured on objective benchmarks instead of subjective win-rates. Finetuning base Llama-3, Mistral and Llama-2 (7B-70B) on 0-10,000 task examples, post-training accuracy follows a power law P proportional to D^(1/b) in the number of examples across math (GSM8k), coding (HumanEval+), instruction following (IFEval) and multihop QA (SubQA) - it does not saturate after a handful of examples, and bigger base models improve at an increasing rate. An error breakdown shows style/formatting saturates within ~100 examples while reasoning keeps improving (total mistakes correlate with reasoning errors at r2=0.98 math / 0.99 multihop on Llama-3-8B), and win-rate is shown to be actively misleading: a style-tuned LIMA-1k model wins 84.4% of GPT-4o head-to-heads on GSM8k while solving only 14.7% of problems, versus a 46.5%-accurate task-tuned model that wins 0.24%. Also shows post-training for reasoning helps a model integrate and use knowledge from beyond its pre-training cutoff (Facts100).

papers.title: Planning In Natural Language Improves LLM Search For Code Generation
papers.authors: Wang, Cassano, Wu, Bai, Song, Nath, Han, Hendryx, Yue, Zhang
papers.abstract: Argues LLMs gain little from repeated sampling because instruction/preference post-training collapses output diversity, so the model re-samples near-identical wrong solutions - the right place to search is idea space, expressed in natural language. PLANSEARCH builds a depth-2 tree of combinatorial 'observations' (all subsets of size <=2 of 3-6 first-order hints, expanded once more into second-order hints), turns each leaf into a natural-language plan plus an adversarial 'assume the idea is wrong' variant, then translates plan -> pseudocode -> Python, yielding ~300-400 candidates per problem. On contamination-controlled LiveCodeBench (May-Sept 2024 problems, after Claude 3.5 Sonnet's cutoff) it lifts Sonnet from a 41.4% best no-search pass@1 to 77.0% pass@200, beating repeated sampling and IdeaSearch across GPT-4o-mini/GPT-4o/DeepSeek-Coder-V2/Sonnet on LiveCodeBench, HumanEval+ and MBPP+; against search-native o1-mini the gap nearly closes (91.3 vs 90.8 pass@200). A single LLM-judged idea-diversity score D = 1 - (similar pairs)/(n choose 2) predicts relative pass@1->pass@200 gains across all models and methods, and public-test filtering shifts the curve ~10x left (pass@20 ~= 77.1%). PLANSEARCH often lowers pass@1 since diversifying ideas dilutes any single one.

papers.title: Chatbot Arena: An Open Platform for Evaluating LLMs by Human Preference
papers.authors: Chiang, Zheng, Sheng, …, Gonzalez, Stoica
papers.abstract: The open, live LLM leaderboard that became the field's de-facto scoreboard: users prompt two anonymous models with anything, vote for the better answer, and identities are revealed only after voting. By Jan 2024 it had collected ~240K pairwise votes (243,329 conversations) from ~90K users over 50 models and 149 languages - a fresh, real-world prompt distribution that static benchmarks cannot contaminate. The statistical core converts noisy A-beats-B outcomes into a ranking via Bradley-Terry coefficients fit by logistic regression with multiplicity-corrected confidence intervals, plus an active sampling rule that concentrates votes on close match-ups (up to ~54% fewer votes for a target precision) and a sequential test that flags anomalous voters. Validation shows crowd-vs-expert agreement of 72.8-83.1% (near the 79.4-89.8% expert-vs-expert level) and BERTopic finds ~600 long-tailed prompt clusters - but the trust check covers only 320 battles across two lopsided pairs, and the headline Elo, lacking ground truth, tracks human preference (length and style included) rather than contamination-proof capability.

papers.title: Learning Goal-Conditioned Representations for Language Reward Models
papers.authors: Nath, Slack, Da, Ma, Zhang, Whitehead, Hendryx
papers.abstract: Trains reward models with a contrastive, goal-conditioned auxiliary loss alongside the usual Bradley-Terry objective: representations of intermediate states are pulled toward future states on preferred trajectories and pushed away from dispreferred ones, so the reward model's hidden states approximate a Q-function - with no extra annotation. This lifts reward-score AUROC by up to 0.09 on GSM8K/MATH (and +2.3% accuracy on Helpful-Harmless), and the learned Q-values localize the exact step a math solution goes wrong, filter low-value trajectories to improve majority voting while saving up to ~55% of tokens, and steer Llama-3 at decode time toward more helpful generations (+9.6% to +21.6% GPT-4 win-rate vs SFT). Downstream PPO gains are smaller (+1.7% weighted), and the strongest results sit on verifiable-answer math and an LLM-judge win-rate.

papers.title: The WMDP Benchmark: Measuring and Reducing Malicious Use With Unlearning
papers.authors: Li, Pan, Gopal, Yue, Berrios, …, Wang, Hendrycks
papers.abstract: A public benchmark of 3,668 four-choice questions probing hazardous knowledge across biosecurity (1,273), cybersecurity (1,987), and chemistry (408) — a measurable proxy for malicious-use risk that was filtered to exclude operationally sensitive, export-controlled detail. It doubles as an unlearning benchmark and introduces RMU (Representation Misdirection for Unlearning), which scrambles a model's internal representations of hazardous content to drop WMDP accuracy toward the 25% random floor (Zephyr-7B Bio 63.7→31.2) at ~1 MMLU point of cost. Robust to jailbreaks and linear probes, but the unlearning is reversible by finetuning.

papers.title: LLM Defenses Are Not Robust to Multi-Turn Human Jailbreaks Yet
papers.authors: Li, Han, Steneker, Primack, Goodside, Zhang, Wang, Menghini, Yue
papers.abstract: Expert human red teamers running multi-turn conversations break four state-of-the-art LLM defenses (RR, LAT, DERTA, CYGNET) at 51.7–87.9% attack success on HarmBench — defenses that report single-digit (even ~0%) ASR against automated single-turn attacks — beating the best-case automated ensemble by +19.6% to +65.4%. The same humans recover 51.2% of "unlearned" WMDP-Bio knowledge from an RMU model (vs 25.6% for the ensemble), showing machine unlearning obfuscates rather than removes. 91.62% of successful jailbreaks needed more than one turn. Released as the MHJ dataset: 2,912 prompts across 537 multi-turn jailbreaks, with a 7-tactic taxonomy from commercial red-teaming.

papers.title: A Baseline Analysis of Reward Models' Ability To Accurately Analyze Foundation Models Under Distribution Shift
papers.authors: LeVine, Pikus, Chen, Hendryx
