papers.abstract: Stress-tests an off-the-shelf RLHF reward model (OpenAssistant's DeBERTa-v3-large, evaluated on Summarize-from-Feedback) under distribution shift, decomposing prompt-shift from response-shift via both machine-translation (lingual) and word-perturbation. Reward models break asymmetrically: OOD responses crater reward accuracy (72.3%→65.7%) and calibration (ECE 14.5%→20%), while OOD prompts barely register — even fully random ones. Calibration follows a novel paradigm: overconfident and badly miscalibrated near-OOD (ECE peaks ~0.25), then deceptively 'well-calibrated' far-OOD as the binary forced choice collapses to chance. Adapts the classification energy score — using only the two reward logits, no internal activations or training set — to flag when an RM is off-distribution (AUROC→~1.0 for response shift, a weak ~63% for prompt-only shift).

papers.title: From Crowdsourced Data to High-Quality Benchmarks: Arena-Hard and BenchBuilder Pipeline
papers.authors: Li, Chiang, Frick, Dunlap, Wu, Zhu, Gonzalez, Stoica
papers.abstract: An automated pipeline (BenchBuilder) that mines crowdsourced chat logs — Chatbot Arena, WildChat — for the hardest open-ended prompts and grades answers with an LLM judge, yielding Arena-Hard-Auto: a 500-prompt benchmark that separates frontier models better than Chatbot Arena itself (87.4% vs 85.8% of pairs) and tracks its human rankings at up to 98.6% correlation for ~$20 per model. Introduces confidence-aware separability/agreement and a Pair-Rank Brier score for judging whether a benchmark is any good.

papers.title: GLUE: A Multi-Task Benchmark and Analysis Platform for Natural Language Understanding
papers.authors: Wang, Singh, Michael, Hill, Levy, Bowman
papers.abstract: Introduces GLUE, a model-agnostic benchmark that collapses nine recycled English NLU tasks (CoLA, SST-2, MRPC, STS-B, QQP, MNLI, QNLI, RTE, WNLI) into a single macro-averaged leaderboard score, with privately-held test labels on four tasks and a hand-built linguistic diagnostic suite. It deliberately mixes data-rich and data-scarce tasks so that strong scores require sharing linguistic knowledge across tasks rather than per-task fitting; the best 2018 baseline, a multi-task BiLSTM with attention and ELMo, reaches only 70.0, and the best diagnostic score is R3 28 vs human 80, with failures concentrated in logic (negation, monotonicity) because models lean on lexical and subsequence shortcuts. GLUE established the single-number multi-task leaderboard template later inherited by MMLU, BIG-bench and HELM, and saturated within a year, prompting SuperGLUE.

papers.title: SuperGLUE: A Stickier Benchmark for General-Purpose Language Understanding Systems
papers.authors: Wang, Pruksachatkun, Nangia, Singh, Michael, Hill, Levy, Bowman
papers.abstract: The harder sequel to GLUE, built after pretraining (BERT, XLNet) pushed GLUE's single-number score past the non-expert human level within a year. SuperGLUE keeps the format — eight English tasks on public data, one macro-averaged leaderboard score, a held-out linguistic diagnostic — but swaps in harder, more diverse tasks (BoolQ, CB, COPA, MultiRC, ReCoRD, RTE, WiC, WSC) spanning QA, coreference and word-sense disambiguation, with crowd-sourced human baselines on every task, an analysis toolkit (jiant), and submission caps. A BERT-large baseline reaches 69.0 and BERT++ (STILTs-style intermediate fine-tuning) 71.5, against an 89.8 human estimate — a ~18-point gap, 35.7 points on WSC. The headroom didn't last: T5-11B hit 88.9 within a year and models soon passed the human baseline, making SuperGLUE the canonical evidence that static NLU leaderboards with a fixed human ceiling saturate against scaling — the result the living-benchmark and held-out-eval wave (MMLU, BIG-bench, HELM, Dynabench) reacted to.

papers.title: DS-1000: A Natural and Reliable Benchmark for Data Science Code Generation
papers.authors: Lai, Li, Wang, Zhang, Zhong, …, Fried, Wang, Yu
papers.abstract: A thousand data-science coding problems harvested from StackOverflow across seven Python libraries (pandas, numpy, matplotlib, scikit-learn, scipy, pytorch, tensorflow), each rewritten into an executable context and graded by a multi-criteria execution metric — functional test cases plus surface-form constraints (e.g. banning for-loops to force vectorization) — that accepts a wrong Codex-002 program only 1.8% of the time, versus a reported 60% false-positive rate on APPS. Its signature contribution is a proactive defence against memorization: every problem is perturbed away from its StackOverflow source, after a pilot shows Codex-002's accuracy on the public numpy-100 set collapsing from 72.5% to 23.6% under semantic perturbation (and parroting the original answer 36% of the time). Hand-built by five experts over ~1,200 hours, 1,000 problems from 451 unique sources. The best 2022 system, Codex-002 with infilling, reaches only 43.3% (range 7.4–43.3% across five Codex/InCoder/CodeGen models); infilling beats left-to-right by +4.1% and skill doesn't transfer across libraries (30.1% on pandas vs 57.0% on matplotlib).

papers.title: Can LLM Already Serve as A Database Interface? A BIg Bench for Large-Scale Database Grounded Text-to-SQLs
papers.authors: J. Li, Hui, Qu, B. Li, Yang, … Cheng, Y. Li
papers.abstract: The text-to-SQL benchmark that moved the field from clean academic schemas to 95 large, dirty, cross-domain databases (33.4 GB, 549K average rows), pairing 12,751 questions with hand-annotated external-knowledge ‘evidence’ in four categories (numeric reasoning, domain knowledge, synonym, value illustration) and a first-of-its-kind efficiency metric, the Valid Efficiency Score (VES). Its headline gap — GPT-4 at 54.89% execution accuracy vs 92.96% for human experts on the hidden test set — became the standard motivation for real-world text-to-SQL, though the model number assumes oracle knowledge: strip the evidence and GPT-4 falls to 34.88% (a +20-point swing that pins grounding, not parsing, as the bottleneck).

papers.title: QuestBench: Can LLMs ask the right question to acquire information in reasoning tasks?
papers.authors: Belinda Z. Li, Been Kim, Zi Wang
papers.abstract: Formalizes underspecified reasoning as a constraint-satisfaction problem and tests whether LLMs can name the single missing fact they need to ask about. Across Logic-Q, Planning-Q, and GSM-Q/GSME-Q (~39k 1-sufficient, multiple-choice tasks), frontier models (as of 03/2025) saturate the math domains (up to 98%) but score only 40-50% on logic and planning. A filtered ablation shows that being able to solve the well-specified problem does not transfer to asking the right question, and on planning models fall below a random baseline at even detecting that information is missing.

papers.title: MathDial: A Dialogue Tutoring Dataset with Rich Pedagogical Properties Grounded in Math Reasoning Problems
papers.authors: Macina, Daheim, Pal Chowdhury, Sinha, Kapur, Gurevych, Sachan
papers.abstract: A 2,861-dialogue corpus of one-to-one math tutoring, built by pairing 91 expert teachers with an LLM that role-plays a confused student over GSM8K problems and annotating each turn with a four-move scaffolding taxonomy (Focus / Probing / Telling / Generic). Its signature contribution is the Success@k / Telling@k pair that makes the guide-vs-tell tradeoff measurable: un-prompted ChatGPT reveals the answer 66% of the time, while a 780M Flan-T5 finetuned on MathDial matches its solve rate at under 1% telling. The pedagogical-dialogue substrate later tutoring benchmarks (MRBench, TutorBench) build on.

papers.title: HellaSwag: Can a Machine Really Finish Your Sentence?
papers.authors: Zellers, Holtzman, Bisk, Farhadi, Choi
papers.abstract: A commonsense sentence-completion benchmark built by Adversarial Filtering: a strong generator (GPT-1) oversamples wrong endings, a discriminator (BERT-Large) iteratively keeps only the ones it cannot catch, and humans validate — yielding 70k four-way questions trivial for people (95.6%) yet beating every 2019 model (BERT-Large 47.3%, a >50-point zero-shot gap). The signature 'Goldilocks zone' tunes difficulty by generation length (~2 sentences, ~41 tokens). Its famous log-linear extrapolation — 100k GPU-years to reach human level — was beaten by GPT-4 (~95%, 10-shot) within four years; the dataset built to show machines cannot finish your sentence became the field's default LLM scaling benchmark.

papers.title: Prometheus 2: An Open Source Language Model Specialized in Evaluating Other Language Models
papers.authors: Kim, Suk, Longpre, Lin, Shin, Welleck, Neubig, Lee, Lee, Seo
papers.abstract: The strongest open LLM-as-a-judge of its era, built on a deliberately simple trick: train one evaluator for direct 1–5 scoring (on the Feedback Collection) and a second for pairwise A/B preference (on the new Preference Collection — the first pairwise dataset with 1K+ instance-wise custom criteria), then merge their weights with DARE-Linear into a single model that does both formats. Prometheus-2-8x7B (from Mixtral) and 7B (from Mistral) beat prior open evaluators (Prometheus, Auto-J, PairRM) by at least 0.2 Pearson on direct assessment and win all four pairwise benchmarks — even beating PairRM and Auto-J on their own in-domain sets — halving the gap to GPT-4/Claude. Merging across formats beats joint training (which transfers negatively) and isn't mere ensembling: merging two same-format models hurts. The honest caveat: 'matches GPT-4' is measured by correlation with GPT-4/Claude/humans on partly GPT-4-distilled, partly in-domain data, and the residual gap to human judgment (FLASK r=0.555 vs GPT-4's 0.679) is real.

papers.title: MMMU: A Massive Multi-Discipline Multimodal Understanding and Reasoning Benchmark for Expert AGI
papers.authors: Yue, Ni, Zhang, Zheng, …, Sun, Su, Chen
papers.abstract: A multimodal benchmark of 11.5K college-exam questions across 6 disciplines, 30 subjects and 30 heterogeneous image types (diagrams, chemical structures, music scores, medical scans), built to test expert-level perception, domain knowledge and deliberate reasoning toward “Expert AGI.” At release the best open LMMs scored ~34% and GPT-4V 55.7% on the 10.5K-question test set, versus 88.6% for (open-book) human experts; tellingly, GPT-4 with no image input reached 33.8%, matching vision models and exposing how text-solvable — and how vision-underused — early multimodal models were. Evaluation is zero-shot micro-averaged accuracy with rule-based answer extraction (unparsed multiple-choice answers are filled at random). Now the de-facto multimodal leaderboard (later validation scores: Gemini 1.5 Pro 62.2, GPT-4o 69.1).

papers.title: MathVista: Evaluating Mathematical Reasoning of Foundation Models in Visual Contexts
papers.authors: Lu, Bansal, Xia, …, Galley, Gao
papers.abstract: A 6,141-example benchmark for mathematical reasoning in visual contexts, fusing 28 existing multimodal datasets with three new ones (IQTest, FunctionQA, PaperQA) across 5 tasks and 7 reasoning types. Of 12 foundation models, GPT-4V leads at 49.9% — beating Multimodal Bard by 15.1 points but trailing humans (60.3%) by 10.4 — and collapses on logical reasoning and numeric commonsense, with an error study attributing ~half of failures to hallucinated visual facts. Its LLM-as-answer-extractor scoring pipeline and withheld-label test split became a template for later multimodal-reasoning evals.

papers.title: DEBAGREEMENT: A comment-reply dataset for (dis)agreement detection in online debates
papers.authors: Pougué-Biyong, Semenova, Matton, Han, Kim, Lambiotte, Farmer
papers.abstract: A 42,894-pair Reddit dataset (five socio-political subreddits) for agree/neutral/disagree detection, annotated by 529 Scale AI workers via dynamic consensus and shipped with a temporal user-interaction graph — built to be messy and context-rich where prior stance datasets are small, formal, and text-only. Fine-tuned encoder LMs (BERT/RoBERTa/DeBERTa/XLNet) all plateau at 62–64% accuracy and fail hardest on neutral interactions (44.1% recall); a model trained on the formal Perspectrum dataset hits 90.5% there but drops below the majority baseline on Reddit, showing messy and formal (dis)agreement share little transferable signal.

papers.title: Learn to Explain: Multimodal Reasoning via Thought Chains for Science Question Answering
papers.authors: Lu, Mishra, Xia, Qiu, Chang, Zhu, Tafjord, Clark, Kalyan
papers.abstract: ScienceQA is a 21,208-question multimodal multiple-choice benchmark drawn from grade 1-12 science curricula (3 subjects, 26 topics, 127 categories, 379 skills) and is the first at scale to annotate every answer with a lecture (general background) and an explanation (the specific reason). That turns it into a testbed for chain-of-thought: models are trained to emit the answer followed by the lecture and explanation. Few-shot GPT-3 (text-davinci-002) with CoT reaches 75.17% and fine-tuned UnifiedQA gains +3.99% to 74.11%, against 61.87% for the best native VQA model and 88.40% for humans; CoT explanations meet a human gold bar 65.2% of the time, and feeding gold explanations into the prompt lifts accuracy to 94.13%. Caveats: the strongest models read a generated image caption rather than pixels, and the few-shot accuracy gain from CoT is only ~1 point. A direct ancestor of the multimodal-reasoning benchmark lineage (MathVista, MMMU, OlympiadBench).

papers.title: G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using GPT-4 with Better Human Alignment
papers.authors: Liu, Iter, Xu, Wang, Xu, Zhu
papers.abstract: G-Eval scores generated text with a frozen LLM (GPT-4 / GPT-3.5): a prompt states the criterion, the model auto-writes its own chain-of-thought evaluation steps, and then fills in a score form whose output-token probabilities are weighted into a continuous metric (score = Σ p(sᵢ)·sᵢ). GPT-4, which exposes no logprobs, is sampled 20 times at temperature 1 to estimate that distribution. Reference-free, G-Eval-4 reaches 0.514 Spearman with human ratings on SummEval — beating the trained SOTA UniEval (0.474) and GPTScore (0.417) — with its largest margin on abstractive factual consistency (QAGS-XSum). It also surfaced the now-canonical warning that LLM judges prefer LLM-written text, a self-reinforcement risk if the metric is used as a reward signal — though on thin evidence (human inter-annotator agreement α=0.07). The paper that fixed the modern LLM-as-judge recipe: CoT-guided, form-filling, probability-weighted.

papers.title: MultiLoKo: a multilingual local knowledge benchmark for LLMs spanning 31 languages
papers.authors: Hupkes, Bogoychev
papers.abstract: A 31-language benchmark whose questions are sourced locally per language (500 closed-form Wikipedia questions each, filtered for local relevance) rather than translated from English, then made fully parallel via human and machine translations. Across 11 multilingual-marketed models none scores well (best average EM ~34), knowledge transfers poorly across languages, and swapping locally-sourced for English-translated data can shift a single language's score by up to 22 points while leaving model rankings intact.

papers.title: BLEU: a Method for Automatic Evaluation of Machine Translation
papers.authors: Papineni, Roukos, Ward, Zhu
papers.abstract: BLEU scores a machine translation by its modified n-gram precision — candidate n-gram counts clipped to the maximum count in any single reference — combined as the geometric mean over 1- to 4-grams and multiplied by a corpus-level brevity penalty that stands in for recall. Validated on five systems (two human translators, three commercial MT) over a 500-sentence Chinese→English news corpus, it correlates 0.99 with monolingual and 0.96 with bilingual human judgments. The first cheap, fast, language-independent, reusable MT metric — it unblocked the MT research loop and became NLP's default (and most over-trusted) automatic yardstick.

papers.title: Empirical Analysis of the Strengths and Weaknesses of PEFT Techniques for LLMs
papers.authors: Pu, Jain, Yin, Kaplan
papers.abstract: A uniform head-to-head benchmark of four PEFT methods — LoRA, (IA)³, prompt tuning and BitFit — against full fine-tuning on FLAN-T5-XL (2.85B) across classification/generation tasks and three data scales. The counterintuitive headline: in low/medium-resource settings full fine-tuning converges faster (up to 87% faster on low-resource CoLA) and often scores higher, so parameter-efficient ≠ compute-efficient; the best method depends on data scale (BitFit/LoRA at low data, full tuning as data grows, (IA)³ most memory-frugal, prompt tuning worst). An ablation shows adapting only later layers halves LoRA/(IA)³ trainable parameters while maintaining performance, though (IA)³ is fragile to where you cut and LoRA is robust.

papers.title: METEOR: An Automatic Metric for MT Evaluation with Improved Correlation with Human Judgments
papers.authors: Banerjee, Lavie
papers.abstract: METEOR fixes BLEU's blind spots for machine-translation evaluation: it builds an explicit word-to-word alignment — matching on exact form, Porter stem, and WordNet synonym — then scores it with a recall-weighted harmonic mean (recall counted 9× precision), Fmean = 10PR/(R+9P), discounted by a fragmentation penalty for word order (Score = Fmean·(1−Penalty)). On the DARPA/TIDES 2003 Arabic→ and Chinese→English data it tracks human judgment far better than BLEU: at the system level recall alone lifts Pearson R from BLEU's 0.817 to 0.941 and full METEOR to 0.964 (Chinese), and it stays meaningful at the segment level (≈0.33) where BLEU's geometric averaging collapses to zero. Hand-tuned and still pure surface matching, but the diagnosis — recall matters, align explicitly, score per sentence — endured: the pivotal middle node from n-gram counting (BLEU) to learned and LLM-as-judge metrics (G-Eval).

papers.title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
papers.authors: Karpukhin, Oğuz, Min, Lewis, Wu, Edunov, Chen, Yih
papers.abstract: Shows open-domain QA retrieval can be done with learned dense embeddings alone: two BERT encoders trained with an in-batch-negatives contrastive loss on only a few thousand question–passage pairs beat a tuned BM25 by 9–19 points top-20 accuracy (78.4% vs 59.1% on Natural Questions) and set new end-to-end exact-match SOTA on 4 of 5 QA benchmarks (41.5 EM on NQ vs ORQA's 33.3), with a DPR trained on just 1,000 pairs already beating BM25. The in-batch-negative dual encoder became the default first stage for open-domain QA and the retriever behind RAG; later work (BEIR) showed its zero-shot out-of-domain generalization is weaker than the in-domain gains imply.

papers.title: LAB-Bench: Measuring Capabilities of Language Models for Biology Research
papers.authors: Laurent, Janizek, Ruzo, Hinks, Hammerling, Narayanan, Ponnapati, White, Rodriques
papers.abstract: A 2,457-question multiple-choice benchmark of practical biology-research tasks - literature search (LitQA2, SuppQA), figure and table reading (FigQA, TableQA), database lookup (DbQA), protocol troubleshooting (ProtocolQA), and DNA/protein sequence and cloning work (SeqQA, CloningScenarios) - meant to test whether frontier LLMs can assist research rather than just answer textbook questions. Every question carries an explicit 'decline to answer' option, so models are scored on accuracy, precision, and coverage; tool-free 2024 frontier models trail PhD-expert humans on most categories, and an open-response rerun shows much of their multiple-choice precision comes from eliminating distractors rather than reasoning.

papers.title: SimCSE: Simple Contrastive Learning of Sentence Embeddings
papers.authors: Gao, Yao, Chen
papers.abstract: SimCSE learns sentence embeddings with a contrastive objective whose positive pairs come, in the unsupervised case, from encoding the same sentence twice under different dropout masks (removing the dropout noise collapses the representation), and in the supervised case from NLI entailment pairs with contradiction hypotheses as hard negatives. With BERT-base it reaches 76.3% (unsupervised) and 81.6% (supervised) average Spearman correlation on seven STS tasks -- matching prior supervised systems with no labels, and setting a new state of the art with NLI -- and explains the gains via the alignment and uniformity of the embedding space.

papers.title: Long Range Arena: A Benchmark for Efficient Transformers
papers.authors: Tay, Dehghani, Abnar, …, Ruder, Metzler
papers.abstract: A unified benchmark for efficient ("xformer") Transformers: six encoding-only tasks spanning 1K-16K tokens -- ListOps, byte-level text classification and document retrieval, pixel-level image classification, and the Pathfinder/Path-X spatial puzzles -- all trained from scratch with no pretraining, so the score reflects an architecture's inductive bias rather than its data. Across ten models, BigBird (55.01 avg) barely edges vanilla attention (54.39), per-task winners differ, and the fast kernel/low-rank models (Performer, Linformer, Linear Transformer) trade a few LRA points for ~5x speed and ~10x less memory -- while every model fails Path-X (16K) at chance, the benchmark's open frontier.

papers.title: Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage?
papers.authors: Schaeffer, Miranda, Koyejo
papers.abstract: Argues that the celebrated "emergent abilities" of LLMs -- sharp, unpredictable capability jumps at scale -- are largely an artifact of the researcher's choice of metric, not a fundamental change in the model with scale. When per-token cross-entropy falls smoothly (a power law), a nonlinear metric like exact-match Accuracy (all L tokens correct, ~ p^L) or a discontinuous one like Multiple Choice Grade manufactures a cliff; re-scoring the same fixed outputs with a linear metric (Token Edit Distance) or continuous one (Brier Score) reveals smooth, predictable improvement. Three lines of evidence: changing the metric ablates emergence on the InstructGPT/GPT-3 family's arithmetic; >92% of BIG-Bench's claimed emergent abilities sit under just two metrics (Multiple Choice Grade and Exact String Match); and seemingly emergent abilities can be manufactured at will in vision networks across diverse architectures. NeurIPS 2023 Outstanding Paper.

papers.title: VQA: Visual Question Answering
papers.authors: Antol, Agrawal, Lu, Mitchell, Batra, Zitnick, Parikh
papers.abstract: Introduces Visual Question Answering: given an image and a free-form, open-ended natural-language question, produce a short natural-language answer. Unlike image captioning, VQA admits a clean automatic metric -- an answer scores fully correct once at least 3 of 10 human annotators give it -- so progress is measurable. Ships ~0.25M images (204,721 from MS COCO + 50K abstract scenes), ~0.76M questions, and ~10M answers across open-ended and multiple-choice tracks. Its blind (question-only) and image-only baselines exposed strong language priors: a question-only LSTM reaches 48.76% vs 57.75% for the full multimodal model and 83.30% for humans. That self-diagnosed shortcut seeded both the modern multimodal-benchmark lineage (ScienceQA, MathVista, MMMU) and the balanced-pairs correction of VQA v2.
